articles.title: ‘It’s become a nepo baby sport’: how secondhand fashion got so expensive
articles.summary: Thanks to gen Z resellers and platforms like Depop, thrifting has lost its subcultural roots - and ballooned into a $350bn business Vintage boutique owner Janelle Best has been rummaging in thrift shop bins since the 90s. When she started, she says, there was a stigma about it. “It was either you were a freak, or you were poor – and I was a freak.” At first, she did it to source stage outfits while playing in psych-rock bands. Then, in 2005, she started “casually reselling” on eBay. Ten years later she was trading as Desert Stars Vintage at street markets, and by 2023 she had opened a showroom specializing in upmarket silk dresses in her Williamsburg apartment. Even just a few years ago, she says, she still put in the hours at places like Goodwill. The bins, she says, “were a field day. There were so many fucking bangers.” And then, thrifting became a social media craze, she says, “and the hell gates of high school students flooded in. Twelve people to each bin. Everyone fighting. Suddenly everyone’s a reseller, and instead of these kids intuitively knowing what’s good, they’re on their phones trying to figure out if it’s valuable.” Continue reading...
articles.content:
Thanks to gen Z resellers and platforms like Depop, thrifting has lost its subcultural roots - and ballooned into a $350bn business

V intage boutique owner Janelle Best has been rummaging in thrift shop bins since the 90s. When she started, she says, there was a stigma about it. “It was either you were a freak, or you were poor – and I was a freak.”

At first, she did it to source stage outfits while playing in psych-rock bands. Then, in 2005, she started “casually reselling” on eBay. Ten years later she was trading as Desert Stars Vintage at street markets, and by 2023 she had opened a showroom specializing in upmarket silk dresses in her Williamsburg apartment. Even just a few years ago, she says, she still put in the hours at places like Goodwill. The bins, she says, “were a field day. There were so many fucking bangers.” And then, thrifting became a social media craze, she says, “and the hell gates of high school students flooded in. Twelve people to each bin. Everyone fighting. Suddenly everyone’s a reseller, and instead of these kids intuitively knowing what’s good, they’re on their phones trying to figure out if it’s valuable.”

Secondhand clothing has never been more popular. On TikTok, teenage girls post thrift haul videos, gleefully unveiling big bags of clothes acquired for less than $100. Meanwhile, at the top of the fashion food chain, billionaires wear rare vintage pieces to confer status and taste.

Many pin the meteoric rise of secondhand clothing to gen Z, who are reportedly more environmentally aware and entrepreneurial than their elders. The real shift, though, is tech, with platforms such as Depop, the RealReal, Vestiaire Collective, Vinted, ThredUp and Poshmark making secondhand buying and selling more accessible than ever.

The tipping point was the pandemic, when screen time rocketed and buying and selling online felt like a rare way to “hunt for endorphins”, as writer and dedicated thrifter Linnea Pejcha describes her own Depop habits at the time. Thrifting surged globally by 28% between 2021 and 2022, and has kept growing since. Revenue is not confined to the apps: Goodwill, North America’s largest thrift chain, with 3,400 stores across the US and Canada, announced a record $7bn in revenue last year, with plans to open between 50 and 100 new stores across North America. ThredUp projects the market will reach $367bn by 2029 – growing 2.7 times faster than the overall global apparel market.

Theoretically, a boom in reused clothing should be unequivocal good news, given the mountains of fabric sent to landfill each year. But many who have been thrifting for decades are depressed at the changes wreaked on some aspects of the industry as it has mushroomed.

For a start, it’s not just plucky rockers selling vintage now. Even some oligarchs are resellers. Client advisers for top fashion brands, the people who deal with shoppers who are spending hundreds of thousands each year, say that some customers have so much swag that they help them, unofficially, to list their wardrobes on secondhand apps as a sales tactic so they can buy new pieces. If they help them “get rid of some of their wardrobe”, says analyst Claudia D’Arpizio, Bain & Company’s global leader of fashion and luxury, “they unlock a lot of potential new buying”.

A new report by Bain found that half of luxury shoppers consult an item’s price on the secondhand market before deciding whether to buy new. In high-end vintage, certain brands may be lost to all but the wealthiest collectors, says Best. Cavalli pieces, in particular, have rocketed in price “probably five times in value in four years”. A few years ago, she says, “all of these rich girls started hoarding brands like Cavalli and Blue Marine”.

Perhaps none of this is new. Like sex, every generation thinks they invented thrifting, says Jennifer Le Zotte, the author of From Goodwill to Grunge, who teaches at the University of North Carolina, Wilmington. In the past, a lingering sense of stigma – which Le Zotte attributes to antisemitism and germ theory during the earliest days of secondhand clothing, when Jewish pedlars sold clothes from pushcarts in the 19th century – meant thrift felt like “a resource for rebellion, part of an alternative mindset”. That’s how Le Zotte felt herself when she started doing it, in the grunge era, as did the surrealists in the 1920s, and the Rolling Stones and Jimi Hendrix in the 1960s. “I think every generation for a long time has thought that they’re discovering this, this repository of fashionable possibilities.”

But this vintage boom is different. While it has been great for business in some ways – and has seen celebrities such as Madonna wearing her clothes – Best says it’s still harder to make a living now, with the rise in prices of gas and shipping. Not to mention the wild competition for popular stock – sometimes, she says, vintage hunting feels like “a nepo baby sport”.

Best also has to deal with tariffs. The US market, she says, is now “really dry” so she mainly sources from Italy and France. Yet she has to pay 15% on all imports after Donald Trump got rid of the tariff exemption on imports worth less than $800. She thinks prices are only going to go up: “Because vintage is finite, only so many pieces were made at a certain time, and everyone’s looking for it.”

At the cheaper end of the secondhand market, there are widespread complaints from shoppers about prices, which many say have become unfeasibly high for the low-income shoppers who rely on such stores most. Many of those garments are low quality, poorly made fast fashion. Videos calling out inflated prices, posted with titles such as “ Goodwill what crack are you smoking?? ” have become commonplace. One dedicated Reddit community – ThriftGrift – collates examples, some of which are, frankly, distressing: a Carhartt hoodie covered in stains for $12 , a $6.99 shirt, which might feel like a bargain until you spot the wasp’s nest attached to the yoke. Some items still have the original tag so you can see, for example, that the pair of peach, pleated pants priced at $8.99 in Goodwill were priced at $7.99 new . Last month, Goodwill’s western and northern Connecticut branch seemed to confirm its aspirations with the launch of a Thrift Tour , offering a “private bus” to three stores, 30 minutes of exclusive early shopping, lunch, a custom T-shirt, a 20% off coupon and the chance to “meet new thrifting besties” for $75.

Though the thrift shop workers I spoke to for this feature said that some price rises were associated with inflation – costs of labor, rent, cleaning and display supplies have all zoomed up – much of it is also driven by awareness of the booming resale market. Managers have put cheat sheets of brands to look out for on the wall in backrooms; they use resale prices on eBay or Depop to gauge value; and with the image-searching app Google Lens, they are more likely to find comparable items. The days of finding a bargain at the bottom of a bin might be over.

The secondhand fashion ecosystem is vast. It ranges from high-end consignment operations, which authenticate, market and sell clients’ items on their behalf, and take a share of the profit (the RealReal is one such example; current wares include a grey leather Hermès Birkin for $32,000), to independent thrift stores in tiny towns where a shopper might still find a donated pair of jeans for a few dollars. In between those price points are vintage stores (the word “vintage” most commonly refers to garments at least 20 years old), which can get very expensive too – particularly in fashion-centric urban centres such as New York City, which often stock rare Y2K pieces such as 00s Jean Paul Gaultier and Dior. Online, dozens of for-profit sites specialise in high street brands – such as ThredUp, which will send a bag to your house to fill with clothes, which they will sell on commission , and sites, from Poshmark to Depop to eBay, where sellers tend to retain more of the profits if items sell, but also do more of the legwork. There are independent thrift stores that operate for profit, many of which buy items from customers as well as sell them, or donate all or part of their profits to charity; small chains which run as non- and for-profit; and large thrift store chains. The largest for-profit thrift chain in North America is private equity-backed Savers, which has more than 300 stores in the US and Canada, and plans to open another 26 this year . The behemoths in thrift clothing, though, are non-profits: Goodwill and the Salvation Army, which has 887 US stores .

The big beasts of thrift have their critics. Many of those incensed by high prices call Goodwill “Greedwill” online. Some refer to it as a “corporate thrift store”, despite its philanthropic mission, which centres on helping people get into work, and say that it operates more like a for-profit store than a charity. Central among their complaints is the fact that CEOs and other executives – of which there are many hundreds, because Goodwill is not run centrally, but as 150 autonomous regional operations – earn chunky salaries, usually of about $300,000 to $700,000. In a statement to the Guardian, Goodwill said that, in 2025, it helped “nearly 2 million individuals with job training, placement and other support services”. Some of those job placements were with Goodwill itself, though that too is not without controversy: in 2013 it emerged that Goodwill was using a loophole dating back to 1938 to pay disabled workers as little as 22 cents an hour. Goodwill has said that while this was legal under section 14(c) of the Fair Labor Standards Act, which allows some people with disabilities to be paid under “a special minimum wage certificate”, it believes “the responsible approach” is to eliminate the use of the certificate in its stores.

Some of those frustrated with the increasingly professionalised thrift economy put the blame squarely on individual resellers entirely for rising prices. Sheri Pavlović, a sustainable fashion expert who shares tutorials on upcycling garments as Refashionista Sheri , says that this can sometimes get ugly on the shop floor. “I’ve been in thrift stores and seen twentysomething cuties, pulling stuff off racks and saying: ‘We could sell this on Poshmark,’ and then seen older people go up to them and say: ‘You’re the reason why it’s so expensive!’ And it’s like, are you kidding me? These little girlies here have nothing to do with corporate prices!” Rather, Pavlović blames the “suits” running big thrift chains who “are getting a whiff of this being popular, trending”. So they hike the prices because they believe they can, she argues, not because they have to.

Robert, who runs a mom-and-pop thrift store in Monterey, California, which serves people on lower incomes, says he has only put its prices up around a quarter of the rate of inflation since the pandemic, despite soaring costs, “because the most important thing for us is customer retention”. He believes the big chains have the scale and brand recognition to charge higher prices, and finds them aggressively competitive: one of them recently put a donation bin near his shop, which has had an impact on donations to his business.

It’s not just resellers skimming off items that may be resold for a profit, either. Many thrift stores have multiple ways of doing the same thing: the practice of “picking” – antique and vintage dealers coming in to buy the best stuff – is well known in the industry, and now many organizations do it for their own websites.

On the price issue, David Eagles, executive vice-president and chief operating officer of Goodwill Industries International, said: “Goodwill does not have one national pricing policy. Prices are set by local Goodwill organizations.” He added: “It is also important to keep the pricing conversation in perspective. We estimate the average retail price per item across the Goodwill network remains below $5. While average item pricing has moved gradually since the pandemic, pricing changes have remained lower than the broader inflationary environment.”

The problems with peak thrift, as this time may become known, extend beyond price. Those donating clothing have enjoyed a sense of virtue from the earliest days of the secondhand clothing market, according to Le Zotte. When Goodwill launched at the turn of the century, its model was tailored to a time of high immigration and wealth inequality. Much like ThredUp today, Goodwill would drop burlap bags at middle class homeowners’ houses, to encourage them to part with their old furniture and clothes which they might then feel free to replace, “without being profligate”.

In an age of fast fashion, though, that sense of virtue appears to be appeasing our guilt about buying new clothing. A recent Yale University study concluded that participation in secondhand fashion often drives, rather than replaces, the purchase of new garments: while 37.2% of people reported increasing clothing donations between 2020 and 2024, there was also a 38% rise in new clothing purchases. This is all a bit at odds with the “moral social buy-in” Le Zotte says many people report feeling when shopping secondhand: “They think that buying the clothing once it enters the secondhand market isn’t being complicit. I’m not saying that it’s totally misguided, but it’s overexaggerated.”

Still, true believers remain. Liisa Jokinen, who runs Gem, an app that aggregates secondhand clothing, insists that “not everything is expensive – but you need to be patient”. She recommends visiting yard sales, clothes swaps, and places where you can still find fill-a-bag sales and shop-by-the-pound stores in person.

Pavlović has abandoned thrifting for now, owing to rising prices, in favor of altering her existing garments to create new items, but she still disagrees with the received wisdom that all the “good” stuff is gone from thrift stores now. “What is good stuff to you is not good stuff to me. I don’t give a shit about the label.” She recommends forgetting the kind of brands shop assistants have been trained to look out for – Zara and Levi’s, for example – and prioritizing personal taste. “When I was thrifting regularly, I was always on the hunt for stuff from the 60s and 70s.”

Pat Noecker, owner of Other People’s Clothes, which has four branches in New York City, got what he describes as his “double PhD in thrifting” when he was touring the US as a musician during the 90s and 00s with bands including Liars, “hitting every thrift store in every town”. He says he keeps prices low for the area (as a guide, Zara pieces are sold for $15.98 to $21.98). His business is “profitable, but no one’s buying yachts”. As a store that buys clothing as well, “we price things to move, otherwise we would need a store the size of a football field”.

He says his clients range from low-income locals from nearby Williamsburg to celebrities, including the band Geese and the first lady of New York City, Rama Duwaji, who comes in to sell as well as to buy. Her taste is “impeccable. She’s super into it. We absolutely love her.” She once dropped off a pair of the mayor’s shoes (Chelsea boots), which Noecker has hung on to: “I don’t wear them but I keep them in my office as a reminder that we have a really awesome mayor, and I send him good luck through those shoes.”

warmUpQuestions: Before reading, what do you think might happen in an article titled "‘It’s become a nepo baby sport’: how secondhand fashion got so expensive"?
Which people, places, or problems do you expect this article to mention?
fillInTheBlanks: The article is mainly about ______.
Thrift is important in this story because ______.
The article mentions goodwill to explain ______.

trueFalse.statement: When she started, she says, there was a stigma about it.
“It was either you were a freak, or you were poor – and I was a freak.” At first, she did it to source stage outfits while playing in psych-rock bands.
Then, in 2005, she started “casually reselling” on eBay.
The article says thrift is unrelated to the main events described.
The article says nothing in the situation affects people, places, or decisions.

readingComprehension: What is the main idea of the article?
What does the article say about thrift?
How is goodwill connected to the main story?
Which paragraph gives the most important detail, and why?
discussionQuestions: What is your opinion about the way the article presents thrift?
What question would you ask after reading the details about goodwill?
How might secondhand change if the situation continues?
exercises.summaryTask: Write a three-sentence summary: one sentence for what happened, one for an important detail from the article, and one for why it matters.

articles.title: Chess: Bodhana Sivanandan, 11, breaks world age record from 38 years ago
articles.summary: Harrow schoolgirl eclipses legend Judit Polgar’s mark as youngest girl ever to defeat a grandmaster rated above 2600, then draws with a former 2700 England’s 11-year-old talent Bodhana Sivanandan scored her best individual result last Saturday, when the Harrow schoolgirl defeated France’s 2628-rated grandmaster Marc’Andria Maurizzi, 19, in the opening round of the Dole Trophy in Aix-en-Provence. Maurizzi was world under-20 junior champion in 2023 and is the current French champion. Sivanandan had beaten two GMs previously, but neither was as eminent as Maurizzi, and her result is a new world age record for a girl to defeat a grandmaster rated over 2600. The Frenchman held the advantage for the first 27 moves but then blundered into a tactic which won his queen for inadequate compensation. Continue reading...
articles.content:
Harrow schoolgirl eclipses legend Judit Polgar’s mark as youngest girl ever to defeat a grandmaster rated above 2600, then draws with a former 2700

England’s 11-year-old talent Bodhana Sivanandan scored her best individual result last Saturday, when the Harrow schoolgirl defeated France’s 2628-rated grandmaster Marc’Andria Maurizzi, 19, in the opening round of the Dole Trophy in Aix-en-Provence. Maurizzi was world under-20 junior champion in 2023 and is the current French champion.

Sivanandan had beaten two GMs previously, but neither was as eminent as Maurizzi, and her result is a new world age record for a girl to defeat a grandmaster rated over 2600. The Frenchman held the advantage for the first 27 moves but then blundered into a tactic which won his queen for inadequate compensation.

The previous record holder was the all-time women’s No 1, Judit Polgar, who defeated GM Lajos Portisch , a former world title candidate but then on the wrong side of 50, in the 1988 Hungarian Championship when she was aged 12 years and two months.

At 11 years four months, Sivanandan also became the fifth youngest pre-teen to defeat a 2600+ rated GM, following in the footsteps of Nodirbek Abdusattorov, who did it at nine, Faustino Oro at 10, and Roman Shogdzhiev and Praggnanandhaa Rameshbabu at 11 years one month.

Sivanandan could not maintain her stellar form into last Sunday’s round two, when she lost to the Israeli international master Benny Aizenberg, but in Monday’s third round she delivered another strong result when she drew with GM Maxim Rodshtein, the current Israel No 2. Rodshtein’s peak rating was 2710 a decade ago, and his achievements include winning the Israel Championship and the prestigious Rilton Cup in Stockholm.

After Thursday’s sixth round of nine, Sivanandan had scored three points against mostly much higher rated opponents and was in contention for her second norm (of three needed) for the women’s grandmaster title. She scored her first WGM norm at 10, also a world age record, at the same Dole Open in 2025.

The Quantbox Chennai Grandmasters, which finished on Wednesday, was another success for Alireza Firouzja, who regained his place in the live classical ratings world top 10: Final scores were Firouzja (France) 4.5/7, Dmitry Andreikin (Fide/Russia) and Arjun Erigaisi (India) 4, Abdusattorov (Uzbekistan), Pranesh Munirethinam and Nihal Sarin (both India) all 3.5, Hans Niemann (US) 3, Gukesh Dommaraju (India) 2.

Reigning world champion Gukesh’s disastrous 2026 continued. Earlier in the year, he did poorly in Wijk aan Zee and Prague, then finished last of six in Oslo. Now he has ended up last of eight in his home city of Chennai, failing to win a single game. He has dropped to a record low of 31st in the live ratings with a score of just 2703, barely above the elite base of 2700. He has one more classical event, the Olympiad in Samarkand, Uzbekistan, before his world championship match against his in-form challenger, Javokhir Sindarov.

The omens could hardly be worse, but the world title match will start with a clean sheet, and Gukesh could yet rediscover his 2024 form when he won the Candidates and then the championship match against China’s Ding Liren.

The election for president of Fide will take place in September during the 2026 Olympiad at Samarkand. Nominations close on Sunday and so far there are four candidates:

1: Arkady Dvorkovich, the incumbent Russian who has held the office since 2018, would have been the favourite to be re-elected for a third term, but on Thursday he was personally sanctioned by the EU with a visa ban and an asset freeze due to his alleged role, which he denies, in aiding chess events in Russian-occupied Ukraine. Dvorkovich has now suspended his own presidential functions. The Fide deputy president, India’s ex-world champion GM Viswanathan Anand, will act as interim president. Dvorkovich’s nominated deputy in the election would have been Timur Turlov, president of the Kazakhstan Chess Federation and the billionaire chair of the Nasdaq-listed Freedom Holding, who now has until a 26 July deadline to stand as Fide president. Chess.com has an article on the implications for international chess of the latest developments.

2: Jan Henric Buettner, the German multi-millionaire founder of Freestyle Chess , with its random placement of back rank pieces. Buettner’s deputy president would be Malcolm Pein, the executive editor of CHESS magazine and England’s international director. The Buettner-Pein ticket backed the winner at last weekend’s European Chess Union elections.

3: Wadim Rosenstein, also a German multi-millionaire and head of WR Holdings, a diversified international company. Rosenstein sponsored Fide’s new Rapid and Blitz team championships and played on the amateur board in the WR Chess teams which won gold in 2024 and 2025. His latest venture is the USA v Uzbekistan rapid and blitz match, which takes place in Miami on Monday and Tuesday.

4: Kirsan Ilyumzhinov, whose self-nomination is a major surprise. The eccentric former ruler of Kalmykia, a semi-autonomous state on the Caspian Sea, was Fide president from 1995 to 2018, but is best remembered for his claim to have been abducted by yellow-clad aliens into their spaceship. The full story of that entertaining incident is narrated in this article in Edward Winter’s highly regarded Chess Notes . Ilyumzhinov’s major platform is to promise to try to persuade the world No 1, Magnus Carlsen, to return to classical chess: “I will begin direct negotiations with Magnus Carlsen personally. His return must not be a privilege granted to one player, but evidence that the authority of the entire World Championship system has been restored. We resolved a similar crisis before: in 2006, under Fide’s auspices, we organised the reunification match in Elista between [Vlad] Kramnik and [Veselin] Topalov, ending years of division. We united the chess world then. We can do so again.”

4034 : 1 b4!! played after six minutes. Black’s c5 knight has no escape, while 1…Bxc3? loses to 2 Qxc3 with mate threats at g7 and h8. Black gave up his queen by 1…Qxb4 2 Nd5! Qxb2 3 Rxb2, but White won on material 11 moves later, and so broke Judit Polgar’s world age record. The complete game is here .

articles.title: Are multi-way clothes the answer to sustainable fashion - or just another excuse to buy more stuff?
articles.summary: Brands are making clothes that can be adapted by the wearer. But with fast fashion giants getting in on the act, perhaps we should just stick to wearing our cardigans backwards Don’t get Fashion Statement delivered to your inbox? articles.content:
Brands are making clothes that can be adapted by the wearer. But with fast fashion giants getting in on the act, perhaps we should just stick to wearing our cardigans backwards

I ’m sitting on my bedroom floor trying to fasten the sleeves back on to a new top that’s just been delivered. I haven’t had an unfortunate accident with scissors; the removable sleeves are a design feature, part of the growing trend for multi-way clothing that is being championed as a win for sustainability – or for anyone moving from air-conditioned office to outside right now. Brow furrowed, I try to attach the puff of red taffeta to the rest of the frilly blouse using the tiny buttons and loops provided. But I’m not having much luck …

I enjoy messing around with clothes. Aged 10, I pranced around a sun-drenched Greek holiday in a top that was really a skirt. But a top with sleeves that detach? I’d lose them instantly, I’m sure of it. Even if I didn’t, would my two-tops-in-one actually curb my rabid appetite for new clothes or would it simply feed the beast; another trend to be devoured and then discarded, just like the sleeves?

Multi-way clothing – clothing designed to be worn in multiple ways – has come a long way since the zip-off trousers made popular by practical hiking dads (as well as girl band TLC) in the early 2000s, or the “infinity” bridesmaid dresses that could be worn 72 different ways and were chosen by indecisive (or, perhaps, size-inclusive) brides of the 2010s.

Today, a number of self-proclaimed slow and sustainable brands, including BUCI NYC, Isabella Vrana, Damson Madder and American brand SAUV – the idea for which came about when founder-sisters Margaux and Euxie were forced to split their shared wardrobe as they headed to separate colleges – are creating clothes built to be altered by their wearers. There are reversible cardigans; blouses that can be worn back-to-front; necklines that can be adjusted with a series of hooks and eyes; midi dresses that transform into minis using loops and ribbon ties.

The thinking is that the aesthetic of multi-way clothing – doing more with less – encourages creativity, inspiring the wearer to adapt garments to fit their individual need, occasion or style, while promoting sustainability. Because, of course, the benefit (or so say the brands) is that we will be encouraged to buy less stuff: it is the ultimate sustainable magic trick, to turn one item into two or three.

For Emily Chan, senior sustainability and features editor at British Vogue, the idea of multi-way clothing is to be celebrated, “if it encourages people to style their garments in different ways, get more wear out of them, and keep them for longer”.

One fan is Jasmain Farara, a 29-year-old social media manager from London. In a clip on her TikTok account, she can be seen undoing the poppers on the full-length sleeves of her plain white SAUV top, transforming it, in seconds, into a simple sleeveless vest. It’s marketed as “the last top you’ll ever buy”, though I asked if she has, in fact, bought more tops since and – spoiler alert – she has.

For $135 (about £101), these sets – and other multi-way clothes like them – don’t necessarily come cheap, in case anyone thought the cost of living crisis had led to their boom. For that price, shoppers get the 94% organic cotton popper-sleeved vest as a base and one set of detachable short-capped sleeves. Modular fans can then purchase, separately, other types of sleeves with which to build different tops, like the sartorial version of adding your own toppings to an ice-cream sundae: basic long sleeves for $62 (about £47), for example, or sleeves with a flounce at the cuff for $60 (about £45).

But for all of its good intentions, I fear multi-way clothing may, by its very nature, be paradoxical: buy it so you buy less. There is a crushing inevitability, too, to the fact that fast fashion behemoth ASOS is now getting in on the action , selling a (95% viscose) top with a modifiable neckline that can be made wider or narrower with buttons, for just £24. This isn’t slow and sustainable, it’s a mass-market cash grab, latching on to the aesthetics of multi-wearability and its eco-friendly optics.

Fashion designer and author Orsola De Castro, who co-founded Fashion Revolution, a movement that campaigns for a more sustainable fashion industry, believes multi-way clothing is nothing more than a marketing ploy. “Sustainability-wise, they have absolutely zero merit. They don’t discourage [consumers] from buying new things,” says De Castro. “It is a gimmick; just an added layer that makes you consider the longevity of your clothing but doesn’t necessarily dictate your behaviour.”

Chan agrees when it comes to trend-driven brands jumping on the bandwagon: fast fashion is fast fashion, whether or not it’s multi-use. “If clothes are treated as throwaway items and not made with the environment or garment workers in mind, it’s not a sustainable choice,” she says.

None of this, of course, means we can’t wear clothes in a multi-functional way. Creative people have been doing this forever, without brands marketing it to them. “Who hasn’t worn a cardigan fastened at the back instead of at the front; who hasn’t pinched [their] skirt and somehow tucked it under [their] knickers to change its shape,” De Castro says. “To have it as a pre-designed condition somehow can be restricting rather than liberating.”

Back on the bedroom floor, the top is proving fiddly. It’s not long before I discard the sleeves, reuniting them with the top in its packaging, ready to be returned: my life is fiddly enough already. Plus, maybe there is a better way to experiment with multi-way clothing. Only yesterday, in fact, I accidentally wore my dress back to front all day. It cost me nothing – except, perhaps, a little pride.

articles.title: Socceroos star Cristian Volpato allegedly tests positive for cocaine during roadside test
articles.summary: Australia forward allegedly driving almost 50km/h over speed limit 22-year-old was stopped twice for speeding on Anzac Bridge in Sydney Socceroos forward Cristian Volpato has allegedly tested positive for cocaine after being stopped while driving at almost 50km/h over the speed limit in Sydney. Volpato was one of Australia’s breakout stars of the 2026 World Cup but is now back in the spotlight after allegedly driving a BMW Coupe at 109km/h in a 60km/h zone across Anzac Bridge about 1.10am on Friday. Continue reading...
articles.content:
Socceroos forward Cristian Volpato has allegedly tested positive for cocaine after being stopped while driving at almost 50km/h over the speed limit in Sydney.

Volpato was one of Australia’s breakout stars of the 2026 World Cup but is now back in the spotlight after allegedly driving a BMW Coupe at 109km/h in a 60km/h zone across Anzac Bridge about 1.10am on Friday.

The 22-year-old was subjected to a roadside drug test which allegedly returned a positive result for cocaine. A secondary sample of oral fluid was taken for further analysis, while NSW police confirmed it will continue its inquiries.

Volpato was reportedly issued an infringement notice for speeding and has had his international driver’s licence suspended for six months, meaning he cannot drive in New South Wales. He has not been charged with a criminal offence.

Police allege that Volpato was also stopped two hours earlier on Anzac Bridge, west of Sydney’s CBD, and issued an infringement notice after allegedly driving more than 20km/h over the speed limit.

He was subjected to a roadside alcohol breath test at about 11.05pm Thursday, which returned a negative result.

Volpato plays football for Italian club Sassuolo and represented Italy at at under-19 and under-20 level before switching his international allegiance to Australia on the eve of the World Cup.

He made three appearances for the Socceroos at the tournament and provided an attacking threat as the side progressed from the group stage as runners-up before being knocked out by Egypt in the last 32 .

Volpato was born and raised in Sydney and played junior football at Sydney United 58, Sydney FC and Western Sydney Wanderers before moving overseas to join Serie A club Roma.

He left Roma after two seasons to join Sassuolo where he has scored seven goals in three seasons, before making his mark with the Socceroos and shaping up as a key to the national side’s future.

He left Roma after two seasons to join Sassuolo where he has scored seven goals in three seasons, before making his mark with the Socceroos and shaping up as a key to the national side’s future.

articles.title: Play area aims to interest children in rare habitat
articles.summary: A science centre hopes play is the key to making children care about the future of chalk streams.
articles.content:
The future of one of England's most unique habitats could rest with play areas designed for children to interact with the rare environment, a science centre hopes.

More than eight in 10 of the world's chalk streams are found in England, but the current prolonged hot and dry weather puts pressure on the wildlife that calls the rare waterways home.

Winchester Science Centre, in Hampshire, has now created a play zone themed on the local landscape in the hope children will connect with the environments into adulthood.

It comes as the Berkshire, Buckinghamshire & Oxfordshire Wildlife Trust (BBOWT) threw its support behind a new Westminster group hoping to protect chalk streams.

According to the science centre, the chalk stream exhibit allows children to explore "why water flow, temperature and oxygen matter to one of the world's rarest freshwater habitats".

"In the surrounding chalk grassland, they can discover how plants and insects cope with extreme heat, and why shade, varied vegetation and connected habitats can provide vital refuges for wildlife."

Speaking at the Winchester Science Centre, play consultant Amber Ogunsanya-Williams said the new area gave children the chance to "test the world".

"They get to observe, they get to have all of these gorgeous opportunities to engage with one another and they get to test their ideas on their own terms," she said.

"That to me will develop ecologists for the future because they will remember this space and they will remember what it felt like to be able to test their ideas."

Amber Ogunsanya-Williams says the new area gives children the chance to "test the world"

It follows the creation of a new cross-party group of MPs and peers in Westminster, which aims to protect the rare habitats.

Many of the rivers are found in Berkshire, Buckinghamshire and Oxfordshire - with the region's wildlife trust throwing its support behind the new group.

Holly Gray, from BBOWT, said: "This is an exciting step forward in getting chalk streams the recognition they deserve."

"Chalk streams should be the jewel in the crown of our waterways; a unique, internationally rare habitat we are lucky to have on our doorstep, and we should be proud of," she added.

articles.title: Rare night heron spotted at National Trust site
articles.summary: The small bird, which is widespread across Africa, Asia, the Americas and Europe, is seen on camera.
articles.content:
A bird rarely seen in the UK has been caught on camera in Somerset, raising hopes that work to restore freshwater habitats could provide a refuge for it.

The black-crowned night heron is widespread across Africa, Asia, the Americas and Europe but has now been spotted at National Trust's Holnicote Estate, near Minehead.

This comes as conservationists at the estate introduced new freshwater habitats, providing a refuge for the heron, but also water voles, otters, pine martens, and even white-tailed eagles.

Ben Eardley, project manager for Somerset Coast and Countryside, said: "It's rare to see this wetland species in the UK and so exciting that we have observed one on our estate cameras."

Eardly added: "The UK has lost over 97% of its wetlands through drainage and changes in land management. Renewing existing wetlands and identifying opportunities to create new areas will help wildlife thrive, improve water quality, reduce flood risk and create more resilient landscapes for future generations."

The first confirmed breeding of night heron in the UK was recorded in Somerset in 2017, but it is a scarce visitor to the UK.

As its name implies, it is mainly active at night – making it doubly difficult for people to spot.

Holnicote Estate beaver and wetlands project officer Jack Siviter said: "We are continuing to focus on wetland creation through our project work, as well as letting beavers modify and produce these habitats at scale.

"If we can do this successfully, we are likely to see more wetland specialists arrive at Holnicote, and who knows – night herons might continue to appear."

articles.title: Helen Goh’s recipe for pineapple and mango paletas | The sweet spot
articles.summary: These bright, sharp Mexican iced treats are the perfect summer cooler – with a spiky kick The first time I encountered paletas in Mexico, they felt strangely familiar. They reminded me of the iced treats of my Malaysian childhood: fruit-driven, brightly coloured and made less to indulge than to relieve the heat. What struck me most was the flavour logic shared by both countries, where sweetness is often enlivened with salt, chilli, tamarind or citrus, each sharpening the fruit in its own way and making it seem brighter, cooler and somehow more vivid. Paletas are rarely perfectly smooth; little pieces of fruit suspended in the ice give them texture and interest. These two paletas take different paths from the same base: pineapple – sharp, clean and bright with lime and ginger – and mango – sweeter, rounder and more indulgent, which I’ve spiked with chilli, lime and salt. Continue reading...
articles.content:
These bright, sharp Mexican iced treats are the perfect summer cooler – with a spiky kick

T he first time I encountered paletas in Mexico, they felt strangely familiar. They reminded me of the iced treats of my Malaysian childhood: fruit-driven, brightly coloured and made less to indulge than to relieve the heat. What struck me most was the flavour logic shared by both countries, where sweetness is often enlivened with salt, chilli, tamarind or citrus, each sharpening the fruit in its own way and making it seem brighter, cooler and somehow more vivid. Paletas are rarely perfectly smooth; little pieces of fruit suspended in the ice give them texture and interest. These two paletas take different paths from the same base: pineapple – sharp, clean and bright with lime and ginger – and mango – sweeter, rounder and more indulgent, which I’ve spiked with chilli, lime and salt.

Paleta moulds are easy to find online and in many kitchenware shops, but if you don’t have them, small paper cups and wooden sticks work perfectly well, too.

For the pineapple, lime and ginger paletas 600g fresh pineapple flesh , cut into chunks, plus 70g finely diced into 5-7mm pieces 1 quantity base syrup (see above) 1 tbsp lime juice 20g fresh ginger , finely grated Pinch of fine sea salt

For the mango and Tajín paletas 500g ripe mango flesh , cut into chunks, plus 70g finely diced into 5–7mm pieces 1 quantity base syrup (see above) 1½ tbsp lime juice Small pinch of fine sea salt Tajín or other chilli-lime seasoning , to serve

To make the base syrup, place the sugar and 70ml water into a small saucepan on a medium heat and stir until dissolved. Remove from the heat and cool completely.

For the pineapple paletas, blend the pineapple chunks with the cooled syrup, lime juice, ginger and salt until very smooth. Taste – it should seem slightly brighter and sharper than you’d like, as freezing dulls flavour. Pour the fruit puree into the paleta moulds, leaving about 1cm space at the top. Drop a few pieces of the finely diced fruit into each mould, gently nudging them down with a skewer or teaspoon so they’re suspended throughout the mixture. Tap the moulds lightly on the bench to remove air bubbles, then insert sticks and freeze for at least six hours or overnight. To unmould, briefly run the moulds under warm water.

For the mango paletas, blend the mango chunks with the cooled syrup, lime juice and salt until smooth. Taste and adjust if needed: mangoes vary enormously in sweetness and acidity, so you may wish to add extra lime. Drop a few pieces of the diced fruit into each mould, gently nudging them down with a skewer or teaspoon so they are suspended throughout the mixture. Freeze and unmould as above. Sprinkle the Tajín over the mango paletas just before serving; this keeps the flavours bright and distinct.

articles.title: Experience: I hunt for missing hikers in remote mountains
articles.summary: Since I started doing search and rescue five years ago, I have found seven survivors. Sadly, the number I find dead is higher In 2012, I was a student and had arrived in Taiwan from the Czech Republic. I soon discovered something that interested me far more than my studies: the mountains of Taiwan. I would leave the campus to explore untouched trails near Taipei. I was obsessed with old maps, often looking for routes that had vanished from modern ones, and hiking paths that no one had taken for decades. I stayed in Taiwan because of my love for the mountains – there are more than 250 peaks that exceed 3,000 metres. Among the local hiking community, I became known for my speed and knowledge of remote regions. Continue reading...
articles.content:
Since I started doing search and rescue five years ago, I have found seven survivors. Sadly, the number I find dead is higher

I n 2012, I was a student and had arrived in Taiwan from the Czech Republic. I soon discovered something that interested me far more than my studies: the mountains of Taiwan. I would leave the campus to explore untouched trails near Taipei. I was obsessed with old maps, often looking for routes that had vanished from modern ones, and hiking paths that no one had taken for decades.

I stayed in Taiwan because of my love for the mountains – there are more than 250 peaks that exceed 3,000 metres. Among the local hiking community, I became known for my speed and knowledge of remote regions.

In October 2021, after a typhoon, I heard about a man who had gone missing. I decided to look for him. I hiked for 13 hours, but had no luck. When the man had been missing for more than a week, I received a message from his brother. He had heard about me and asked if I would carry out a search. The fire department was also looking for him.

Over the next three days, I covered 67km with a 6,800-metre elevation gain. I moved back and forth across the peaks, searching for any sign of life. After two weeks, the fire department called off the search.

When he’d been missing for 29 days, the family began to lose hope. We decided on one final attempt. This time, I knew I was close. I started finding fragments of fabric and discarded gear. I was terrified.

When I finally found him, the scene was harrowing. His body was wedged between rocks in the water; he had been dead for weeks. He was an experienced hiker but had underestimated the weather. A stream crossing must have turned fatal, washing him into a side creek where he became trapped between the rocks. I told him I would take him home.

As soon as I got a signal, I called my wife, Eva. She usually delivers the news – good or bad – to the families who hire me. His family gathered eight men, who went with me to bring the body back. We cleared a platform so a helicopter could airlift him out.

The family paid me 300,000 NTD (£7,200) to thank me, plus a 15,000 NTD (£360) day rate. They were devastated, but they finally had an answer. Now, each year, when his family returns to the mountain to pay their respects, they stop by my place.

I filmed the search and posted it on YouTube. It went viral. Since then, I’ve received many requests. I don’t always say yes, but I usually step in if the authorities aren’t making progress.

Sometimes, I carry out search and rescue operations alongside the fire department; other times, I continue after they have called off the search.

Every search begins at my computer, poring over maps and analysing data. I ask the family to provide the missing person’s last-known phone signal location and any CCTV footage from the trailhead car park – this information is invaluable.

Eva and I also interview relatives or friends to understand the missing person’s state of mind – whether they were an experienced hiker who took a wrong turn or perhaps an older person who lost their way.

In my time conducting search and rescue, I have found seven survivors. Sadly, the number of those I find dead is higher. Most people die because they don’t have a map downloaded and make the fatal decision to head downhill. The mountains in Taiwan are vertical. Going down into a valley is a death trap. Those who die almost always do so from a fall.

Search and rescue is a side job. My full-time work with Eva is hosting the Formosa Trail , a mountain race in Taiwan. It’s rewarding to share the beauty of Taiwan’s mountains with people from all over the world.

Finding someone alive brings relief, but seconds later, the mind races: how do we extract them? How do we report it to the authorities? It’s similar when finding a body. There’s the initial shock, and then the immediate pivot to logistics: safe evacuation and next steps. The emotional fallout hits me long after I’ve left the mountain.

Doing this has given my life a true sense of purpose. If I save someone or solve a case, it counts for ever.

articles.title: Heatwave anxiety can make you feel 'incredibly helpless' - here's how to manage it
articles.summary: Extreme heat can take a toll on mental health - and young people and women are among the groups most at risk, according to one study.
articles.content:
Mum-of-two Tavia Watts, 36, says the heat leaves her feeling "like the world is literally ending"

At the peak of the June heatwave, the warmest June on record in England, all Tavia Watts could think about was how hot it was in her two- and five-year-old's bedroom - above 30C in some cases.

"During the heatwave, I've got a high level of anxiety all the time and I'm pretty much useless," she told the BBC. "I've had to leave bedtime when my husband reads [my kids] stories to have a little cry and to calm down, because it's just too hot in their room and I'm afraid for them."

Feeling "incredibly helpless", the 36-year-old posted a short reel to her hundreds of followers on Instagram, in which she voiced her inner anxieties. To her surprise, it got more than 18,000 likes and 1,700 reposts.

In the video Tavia, a stay-at-home mum living in Cheltenham, says the heat "feels like the world is literally ending", she's "terrified for [her] children's lives", and that she "wants to scream at the people not taking this seriously".

Watts is not alone. A 2022 study from the University of Bath estimates that about 4.6% of people in the UK have climate anxiety, which is recognised as a form of mental distress by professional bodies and the NHS. Young people and women are among the groups most at risk, according to a study published in 2025.

"Anticipatory anxiety", related to heatwaves in particular, has become more common, say psychologists Dr Caroline Hickman from the University of Bath and Dr Emma Lawrance from the University of Oxford.

"Once people have been through a traumatic event like a flood or a heatwave, they anticipate the next one," Hickman, who offers therapy to individuals experiencing climate anxiety, told the BBC.

In the UK, "now we're getting the repeat, repeat, repeat [heatwaves]. It triggers that sense that 'I can't be safe in the world, and the world can't be trusted'," she added.

In June's heatwave, Hickman said she "had people saying they're going to kill themselves, I've had people saying they're going to terminate pregnancies, I've had people saying that they want to sell their houses and move somewhere cooler".

Watts said that her video "clearly struck a chord with people" - including therapists posting comments about coping mechanisms - and that she wanted to help others deal with climate anxiety.

"It can go one of two ways. You can either just fall into doomism and think, 'What is the point?'" she said.

"Or you can try to do something. I sign petitions during a heatwave; I organise climate community events; I run a free children's clothes swap shop. During the last heatwave, I finally became vegetarian, because then I can tell my kids at least I tried."

Lizzie Ellen was "terrified" when she first learned about climate change in school, but at the time felt like it was "quite far off".

Now, the scorching temperatures of recent weeks have left the 38-year-old from west London "obsessively" reading about the heatwave and constantly checking her weather app.

Ellen, who gave birth to her first child a few months ago, told the BBC she had felt both isolated and worried about keeping her baby cool.

"With the baby, I've been staying indoors more, keeping on top of checking the temperature… so there's that isolation," she said.

"Then there's the future, which is really scary to be honest - thinking about how the heat we experienced back in June could become a daily reality within a very short time, [my baby's] lifetime… and that's pretty miserable, staying indoors all day in the dark."

Lizzie Ellen, 38, says she "obsessively" reads about the heatwave and is constantly checking her weather app

Some have made major life changes in response to their climate concerns.

Ecologist Dr Francis Windram told the BBC that he had moved from Ascot, near London, to Lancaster in the north-west of England, specifically because of the cooler climate.

He still bought a portable air conditioning unit in spring as a precaution, and is concerned about his grandparents and his father living further south in Hertfordshire.

"We're looking at 35C-plus heat in houses that were not designed for it, with people who are not as able to regulate their temperature, and can't necessarily afford to just fit an AC," Windram said.

Francis Windram moved from Ascot, near London, to Lancaster in the north-west of England, specifically because of the cooler climate

Lawrance had some recommendations on how to cope with climate anxiety:

Talk to others: There's often "a huge perception gap between how concerned people are about climate change, and how much they think other people care", said Lawrance - meaning you might wrongly assume that you're in the minority, or even that you're alone.

"Make space to be kind to yourself, to tend to those emotions and know that it's coming from a place of care for the world," she added.

Find a community: Lawrance recommended finding a strong social network who care about the same things, and taking positive action together.

Whether it's planting a garden in your school or putting solar panels on the roof of your business, action can not only help you channel your anxiety into something positive, but can help alleviate the symptoms of anxiety too.

Lawrance cited the Climate Psychology Alliance in the UK for further support, as well as groups like the Resilience Project, the Climate Cares Centre, and the Climate Majority Project.

Keep yourself cool: Your physical health also affects your mental health, and being hot can also make your anxiety and mood worse, Lawrance said - so she recommended making sure to keep yourself physically cool in extreme heat.

"Having a cool shower, putting a cold washer on your neck, making sure you're hydrated - all of those things will help physiologically as well," she said. "There's a lot we can do - we're not powerless."
paragraphs: Mum-of-two Tavia Watts, 36, says the heat leaves her feeling "like the world is literally ending"
At the peak of the June heatwave, the warmest June on record in England, all Tavia Watts could think about was how hot it was in her two- and five-year-old's bedroom - above 30C in some cases.

"During the heatwave, I've got a high level of anxiety all the time and I'm pretty much useless," she told the BBC. "I've had to leave bedtime when my husband reads [my kids] stories to have a little cry and to calm down, because it's just too hot in their room and I'm afraid for them."

Feeling "incredibly helpless", the 36-year-old posted a short reel to her hundreds of followers on Instagram, in which she voiced her inner anxieties. To her surprise, it got more than 18,000 likes and 1,700 reposts.

In the video Tavia, a stay-at-home mum living in Cheltenham, says the heat "feels like the world is literally ending", she's "terrified for [her] children's lives", and that she "wants to scream at the people not taking this seriously".

Watts is not alone. A 2022 study from the University of Bath estimates that about 4.6% of people in the UK have climate anxiety, which is recognised as a form of mental distress by professional bodies and the NHS. Young people and women are among the groups most at risk, according to a study published in 2025.

"Anticipatory anxiety", related to heatwaves in particular, has become more common, say psychologists Dr Caroline Hickman from the University of Bath and Dr Emma Lawrance from the University of Oxford.
"Once people have been through a traumatic event like a flood or a heatwave, they anticipate the next one," Hickman, who offers therapy to individuals experiencing climate anxiety, told the BBC.
In the UK, "now we're getting the repeat, repeat, repeat [heatwaves]. It triggers that sense that 'I can't be safe in the world, and the world can't be trusted'," she added.

In June's heatwave, Hickman said she "had people saying they're going to kill themselves, I've had people saying they're going to terminate pregnancies, I've had people saying that they want to sell their houses and move somewhere cooler".
Watts said that her video "clearly struck a chord with people" - including therapists posting comments about coping mechanisms - and that she wanted to help others deal with climate anxiety.

articles.title: Australia swimmers banned from thanking Gina Rinehart for Commonwealth Games medal bonuses
articles.summary: Swimmers to earn $30,000 per world record, $20,000 for a gold medal Commonwealth Games Australia gives each team member $5,000 Australia’s Commonwealth Games team is under strict instructions to make no mention of billionaire Gina Rinehart or other private benefactors in media interviews or on social media until after the 2026 Glasgow Games are closed. The warning comes after Rinehart offered up cash bonuses to Australia’s swimmers for winning medals and breaking world records at the Commonwealth Games. Continue reading...
articles.content:
Australia’s Commonwealth Games team is under strict instructions to make no mention of billionaire Gina Rinehart or other private benefactors in media interviews or on social media until after the 2026 Glasgow Games are closed.

The warning comes after Rinehart offered up cash bonuses to Australia’s swimmers for winning medals and breaking world records at the Commonwealth Games.

Individual swimmers will pocket $30,000 for a world record, $20,000 for a gold medal, $15,000 for silver and $10,000 for bronze. Relay team members will be rewarded with $7,500 per person for a world record, $5,000 for a gold medal, $3,750 for silver and $2,500 for bronze.

A Commonwealth Games Australia (CGA) spokesperson said Australia’s best swimmers had a history of always showing up to the Games.

“The opportunity to compete in the green and gold is the biggest incentive in making this happen,” the spokesperson said.

“We commend Mrs. Rinehart for her support of Australian sport, it is exemplary.

“The Australian Team partner family has committed millions of dollars to deliver our team to the Glasgow 2026 Commonwealth Games. Mrs Rinehart and her companies are not Australian Team partners.”

The bonuses being offered by Rinehart are only available to swimmers and would be paid directly to the athletes without any involvement from CGA or Swimming Australia.

The Australian “team membership agreement” includes a clause that prevents all athletes from acknowledging rewards schemes not linked to official Commonwealth Games partners and sponsors, as well as partners of the Australian team, during a blackout period that runs until the Games close on 3 August.

The clause is designed to protect partners and sponsors that support the Australian team directly as well as the embattled Games.

The swimming bonanza comes as many athletes in other sports lament a lack of financial support, despite CGA providing a $5,000 grant to every member of the Glasgow team.

Freestyle king Kyle Chalmers has welcomed the potential windfall for Australia’s swimmer who are well placed to cash in with Canada superstar Summer McIntosh among those skipping the Glasgow Games to focus on the ensuing Pan Pacific championships.

“I’m super grateful to have the support of CGA to get us here, but to be able to have some medal incentive, it gives us a lot more purpose and reason,” sprint star Chalmers said ahead of the opening ceremony. “Otherwise you get a medal and the uniform ... so [I’m] very grateful to have supporters.

“CGA’s made this possible, and the Commonwealth Games internationally being able to put it on, but to have some incentive for all of our hard work is crucial, especially for us older athletes.”

The 28-year-old added Australia’s swimmers are “very lucky” with the level of support they receive compared to some other sports. But the seven-time Commonwealth Games gold medallist insists it remains “very challenging” for many swimmers to juggle arduous training schedules with work and study.

“It’s a choice you make and I guess you kind of understand the financial position of the sport,” Chalmers said. “But fingers crossed there can be change for the next generation to kind of motivate them to continue to make the sacrifices and choose our sport, just to keep those talented athletes in there.”

Rinehart’s bonus scheme was announced by Swimming Queensland, of which the mining magnate is patron, while Australia’s chef de mission Petria Thomas distanced CGA from it.

“That’s a separate arrangement and it’s not something that Commonwealth Games Australia is involved in whatsoever,” former swimmer Thomas said. “Good luck to the swimmers, I guess. They have someone backing that performance, but that’s a separate matter for them.”

The bonuses come on top of annual payments Rinehart makes to many of Australia’s top-tier swimmers. Athletes who win multiple medals will receive a payment for every medal they win.

In a statement, Rinehart said the bonuses are a way of Australia showing appreciation. “There is something very uplifting about seeing an Australian athlete stand on the podium while our flag is raised,” Rinehart said.

articles.title: Scientists find the secret of birdsong's 'spectacular diversity'
articles.summary: A scientific study has revealed the acoustic secret that connects all birdsong - and it is surprisingly simple
articles.content:
The songs of birds like this European wren are shaped by their environment

Scientists in France say they have discovered the building blocks of the incredible diversity of birdsong.

A team analysed more than100,000 songs from 3,000 bird species and revealed that they are all composed of eight fundamental types of sound that the researchers call "motifs".

Each song, they say, is made up of combinations of these motifs - three types of trill, three types of whistle, chaotic notes and harmonies.

Birdsong is one of the natural world's most sophisticated forms of sound-based communication.

Its variety and complexity has fascinated biologists, but with so many different songs, it has been difficult to study on a global scale.

Lead researcher and University of Saint-Etienne PhD student Quentin Bacquelé explained: "The diversity is huge. And if you go to Asia, Africa, Amazonia, you will hear totally different songs."

In a new study, he and his colleagues used a global database of birdsong - recordings uploaded by thousands of volunteers. This meant they could analyse about 116,000 songs from more than 3,000 birdsong species.

They studied patterns in the songs - how each song shifted and changed through time. And with the help of AI, they could then compare the thousands of songs to each other - to find what connected them.

The scientists said this unveiled "a simplicity underlying the spectacular diversity of birdsongs".

A key finding was that the environment the birds evolved in - from tropical forest to temperate landscape - shaped how they "composed" their songs.

So birds adapted for tropical forests use different acoustic building blocks, or motifs, to those from temperate regions.

Simpler motifs, like flat whistles, Bacquelé explained, are often found in tropical environments, apparently because they help the birds make themselves heard through the busy, biodiverse soundscape and dense vegetation.

More complex compositions - with ultrafast trills and combinations that contain lots of information - are more common in temperate forests, where birds tend to communicate at closer range.

It's a kind of evolutionary trade-off: A very complex song provides a lot of information, but it is difficult to transmit that information over long distances in complex environments.

A three-wattled bellbird, a bird from the Central American rainforest whose song carries over long distances

This link between the environment and its natural soundscape, Bacquelé explained, is being transformed by human activity.

"I think this research has opened up a new way to see songs as something [intrinsically] linked with the environment," he told BBC News.

"So, we could try to use these motifs to help us understand how birds could adapt to the new background noise [that we produce]."

Natural sound, Bacquelé added, is an an important part of our own connection to nature and is worth conserving.

"If you think of a forest, near your town or near your house, you think about the songs of the birds in the morning when you go outside.

"And if a bird in a forest has a specific song, if you destroy the forest, then that song may not be adapted any more to the new environment."

articles.title: China's Moonshot AI stole from Anthropic, Trump tech adviser says
articles.summary: The allegations come as Chinese AI companies are facing increased US government scrutiny.
articles.content:
A White House adviser has accused China's Moonshot AI of a "large scale" effort to steal the capabilities of top US artificial intelligence (AI) models.

US President Donald Trump's Science and Technology adviser Michael Kratsios said Moonshot AI carried out the campaign through what is known as distillation - when a weaker AI model extracts answers from a stronger one.

Moonshot also gained access to restricted cutting-edge Nvidia servers to train its models, Kratsios said in a social post , external on Wednesday.

The BBC has contacted Moonshot, Anthropic, the White House, and Nvidia for comment.

Kratsios said on X that the US government has information that Moonshot AI "distilled" capabilities from Anthropic's Fable AI for the development of its K3 model.

Kimi K3 gained attention around the world after it was unveiled last week , with many believing it to have narrowed the gap between Western and Chinese AI models. Moonshot said its K3 model is able to rival top US technology.

Moonshot is likely to have also used servers powered by Nvidia's GB300 Grace Blackwell computing platform, Kratsios said.

Washington restricted the export of Nvidia's most advanced chips in 2022 over concerns that they could be used by the Chinese military.

Since then, government's around the world have cracked down on the smuggling of the chips.

In response to queries from the BBC, the Chinese embassy in Washington referred to remarks made by foreign ministry spokesperson Lin Jian on Wednesday, saying China opposes the "politicising" of trade and tech issues.

Lin said China's AI development is centred on "joint contribution for shared benefit", urging all countries to take a "people-centred approach".

Kratsios' allegations come just a day after Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said on Tuesday the US would examine whether Chinese AI models have stolen the capabilities from American rivals.

On Wednesday, Bessent also said that sanctions "will be on the table" when Chinese companies "cross the line" into intellectual property (IP) theft by conducting "industrial-scale distillation attacks".

"We support open-source AI and the innovation it unlocks. But open source is not open season on American IP," Bessent said on social media.

The Chinese government has not yet issued a public statement in response to the latest allegations but has previously said that its country's development of AI was "the result of its own dedication and effort as well as international cooperation".

The increased scrutiny by the US of Chinese AI companies also comes as Trump is expected to meet his Chinese counterpart Xi Jinping in September.

Moonshot AI will release Kimi K3 as an open-source model on 27 July, making it the first of its scale that can be freely downloaded and customised by developers and members of the public.

In June, Anthropic accused Chinese e-commerce and tech giant Alibaba of "illicitly" extracting the capabilities of its Claude AI models using distillation attacks.

Anthropic urged Congress to penalise the companies behind the alleged attacks and heighten measures to prevent US technology from being stolen.

The White House said in April that it would work more closely with US AI companies to combat "industrial-scale campaigns" by foreign rivals to steal technology.

Kratsios said in a memo at the time that the campaigns were aimed to "systematically undermine American research and development and access proprietary information".

OpenAI says its AI went rogue and launched 'unprecedented' cyber-attack

The White House said in April that it would work more closely with US AI companies to combat "industrial-scale campaigns" by foreign rivals to steal technology.
Kratsios said in a memo at the time that the campaigns were aimed to "systematically undermine American research and development and access proprietary information".
OpenAI says its AI went rogue and launched 'unprecedented' cyber-attack

Moonshot also gained access to restricted cutting-edge Nvidia servers to train its models, Kratsios said in a social post , external on Wednesday.
The BBC has contacted Moonshot, Anthropic, the White House, and Nvidia for comment.

articles.title: Google fined €890m by EU for favouring its own apps over rivals
articles.summary: It is the first major enforcement action against the tech giant under the EU's new digital rules.
articles.content:
Google has been fined €890m (£759m) by the EU for allegedly abusing its power by favouring its own apps and services over those from rivals.

It is the first major action against the tech giant under the EU's Digital Markets Act (DMA), a landmark law aimed at curbing the power of the world's biggest tech companies.

The Commission argued Google's practices limited consumer choice and gave its own services an unfair advantage over rivals.

But the firm criticised the decision, arguing the EU's requirements could damage services used by millions of European customers.

"To comply, we are having to strip away real-time Search features Europeans love - like instant pricing and direct availability for hotels, flights and restaurants - and dismantle safety protections on Google Play," said Google's president of global affairs Kent Walker.

EU officials rejected that argument, saying the measures are necessary to prevent dominant platforms from disadvantaging rivals.

The total fine handed down to Google is comprised of two separate breaches of the DMA.

The European Commission imposed a €460m penalty after finding Google favoured its own services for helping people book flights and hotels over competitors in search results.

And it gave the firm a further €430m fine due to its Play Store rules, with regulators saying it didn't allow people to be shown cheaper offers available outside Google's own marketplace.

Google has a long history of disputes with European regulators, having previously received billions of euros in fines over separate competition cases.

EU competition chief Teresa Ribera said companies should succeed because of the quality of their products - rather than their market position.

"The best products should succeed because they're better, not because they're owned by the company running the search engine," she said.

"After this decision, we want to make sure that there is more competition and also other companies are able to innovate," she said.

Zach Meyers, director of research at the Centre on Regulation in Europe, noted a delay in the competition authority finalising its decision against Google, telling the BBC it was likely driven "by a desire not to upset EU-US relations".

As US president Donald Trump has frequently threatened or imposed new tariffs , however, the Commission came to see its efforts as futile and went ahead with the fine.

"It reflects a growing view among EU officials that the US president's unpredictability and unwillingness to comply with his own deals means that the EU has little to gain by treading softly - and potentially a lot to lose in terms of regulatory credibility," Meyers said.

Google now has 60 days to comply with the regulations - or challenge them by taking the Commission to court.

AliExpress fined record €550m by EU for allowing sale of illegal goods

articles.title: Trump hosts champion Dodgers at White House as some players stay away
articles.summary: Betts and Hernández opt against joining team for event Back-to-back World Series winners gift ring to Trump Many loyal fans had conflicted feelings over visit Donald Trump hosted the World Series champion Los Angeles Dodgers at the White House on Thursday for the team’s second trip to the White House in two years. The team gave the president a custom jersey and a World Series championship ring during a ceremony at the Rose Garden. Four-time MVP Shohei Ohtani, star first baseman Freddie Freeman, World Series MVP Yoshinobu Yamamoto and manager Dave Roberts were among those in attendance, while several players – most notably star shortstop Mookie Betts and utility man Kike Hernández – opted not to attend. Continue reading...
articles.content:
Donald Trump hosted the World Series champion Los Angeles Dodgers at the White House on Thursday for the team’s second trip to the White House in two years.

The team gave the president a custom jersey and a World Series championship ring during a ceremony at the Rose Garden. Four-time MVP Shohei Ohtani, star first baseman Freddie Freeman, World Series MVP Yoshinobu Yamamoto and manager Dave Roberts were among those in attendance, while several players – most notably star shortstop Mookie Betts and utility man Kike Hernández – opted not to attend.

“Special winners, unbelievable,” Trump said. “I would not be surprised to see them back here again.”

In remarks that also included lengthy tangents on former Yankees owner George Steinbrenner and the renovation of the Rose Garden, Trump complimented several Dodgers players while also confusing the roles of some others. He made a lengthy comparison between Ohtani and legendary slugger Babe Ruth, and praised Clayton Kershaw, the Dodgers ace who pitched an inning of last year’s epic World Series Game 3 win over the Blue Jays in 18 innings.

“I watched him beat a lot of teams. They didn’t do too well against him. He is still pitching well, I bet,” Trump said of Kershaw, who retired after last year’s World Series.

Trump said he spoke with Roberts before the event and expressed surprise at the Dodgers’ status this season as the best team in baseball, but noted he’s “been a little busy with Iran and some other things”.

The team gave Trump what appeared to be a replica of their championship ring.

“Wow. Do I have to report this?” he said as he put the ring in his jacket. “I don’t want to report this.”

The Dodgers’ trip to the White House left many of their fans with conflicted feelings, given recent ICE raids and protest crackdowns in the city of Los Angeles as ordered by the Trump administration.

Betts, who did not participate in the Boston Red Sox’s trip during Trump’s first term but was there during the Dodgers’ visit last year, told reporters he preferred to spend the day with his family. Hernández, an outspoken critic of the ICE raids in Los Angeles, said he had a conflicting injury rehab assignment but wouldn’t have participated if he was available to join. Pitcher Edgardo Henriquez also reportedly did not attend.

“My personal line, my company line, is I hope we get this invitation every year because that’s the goal, to win a championship,” Roberts said earlier this year after the team accepted the invitation. “I’m not a politician, and I’m doing something that teams have done for decades. And so that’s where I stand, really. I’m a baseball coach. That’s what I do.”

Teams who have won a major championship have traditionally been invited to the White House to celebrate their victory with the president. During Trump’s first term, several teams were not invited or made clear they would not attend, including champions from the NBA and the WNBA.

This year, the White House has hosted the Olympic gold-winning US men’s hockey team and the NHL’s Florida Panthers to celebrate championships. The Oklahoma City Thunder, the 2025 NBA champions, opted not to visit; the owner of the 2026 champion New York Knicks has said Trump invited the team but there has been no decision made on whether they will attend.
