articles.title: The smartest time to do holiday shopping is in July. We found the best, earliest deals
articles.summary: Christmas shopping in July is better. These 12 very early Black Friday deals, on tried and tested products, make the perfect gifts – for yourself or others Technically, we still have 60 days to enjoy summer. Yet in the world of retail, Black Friday deals have already arrived. To mark the rough half-way point to the holidays, retailers such as Macy’s and Best Buy have rebranded Black Friday as “Christmas in July” discounts. Others such as Nordstrom are running their biggest sales of the year. Most of these sales are only available from now until 26 July. Continue reading...
articles.content:
Christmas shopping in July is better. These 12 very early Black Friday deals, on tried and tested products, make the perfect gifts – for yourself or others

Yet in the world of retail, Black Friday deals have already arrived. To mark the rough half-way point to the holidays, retailers such as Macy’s and Best Buy have rebranded Black Friday as “Christmas in July” discounts. Others such as Nordstrom are running their biggest sales of the year. Most of these sales are only available from now until 26 July.

If, like many Americans, the high cost of living has you wincing at the mere thought of buying holiday gifts, shopping these sales ahead of the busy season may alleviate some of your anxiety.

However, not all of these so-called “doorbuster” discounts are actually worth your money – some are eternally on sale and masquerading as flash deals . For the ones worth shopping, we found items our editors have previously used and recommended, and checked their pricing history to make sure they’re truly good deals.

Here are 12 very early holiday deals so you can stock up on great gifts while they’re steeply discounted – including a bestselling pair of wireless headphones and an upgraded travel pillow . Plus, winter boots and jackets that cost far less out of season, and an adorable teddy bear Christmas tree ornament that’s more than 50% off.

The waterproof shoes recommended by Mirna Valerio, a former National Geographic Adventurer of the Year, are now on sale for 40% off in white . “They’re warm, waterproof and rugged enough for hikes outside in the snow,” she told us in our guide to the best winter boots for women.

Already wishing away the humid weather ? Fast forward to cooler temperatures and grab this winter jacket beloved by Julia O’Malley, an Anchorage local and journalist: “I’m partial to my Patagonia Downdrift Women’s Parka, which is extra warm thanks to its length and 600-fill-power recycled down. Crafted with sturdy four-ply nylon faille, it washes well too,” she writes .

Gift a spa-like experience without the price tag with one of Filter contributor Tobey Grumet Segal’s go-to skincare products as a woman over 50: “Since I’ve been consistently gliding it across my neck, I’ve noticed my skin is tighter and more contoured,” she writes .

My Kindle is the main reason I read roughly 50 books each year. So, if you’re looking for a gift for the person who has lofty reading ambitions, grab one of our favorite e-readers while it’s on sale for $25 off sticker price.

Give your recipient an actually restful sleep on a plane for once with Trtl’s Travel Pillow. It made the cut in our travel essentials guide , where a frequent world traveler shared the products to pack and what to leave behind.

Take the opportunity to make your holiday Pinterest board come to life with these adorable teddy bear ornaments . They’re designed with 95% wool and made in a Fair Trade-certified factory, making them one of our top picks in last year’s guide to sustainable holiday decor .

Famous Footwear is offering a seasonal buy one, get one deal on much of its inventory. A meteorologist recommended these winter boots that you can prep for two recipients for the price of one: “[They] get me through every winter – that includes the ones with 90in of snow in a season – and the long stretches of frigid days. The stability and grip never let me down when I’m shoveling or taking our husky on a walk. They are waterproof, allowing them to survive wet, slushy snow and handle the melting season. I wear these with almost any outfit, including for work, without sounding like I’m clomping around in boots.”

If you need a practical gift for the person who still prefers a phone call to a text , we recommend Shokz OpenFit 2+ Open Ear Buds. “An open-ear design banishes the dreaded cotton-balls-in-your-ears sensation, making these the perfect pick for frequent callers,” writes Simon Cohen, a Filter contributor, in our guide to the best wireless earbuds on the market.

The winter jacket recommended by Tamar Van Delen, a ski instructor, is now on sale for more than $200 off its original price. “It has everything a good ski jacket should have: a snow stopper, a lift pass pocket and multiple inside pockets and it’s super warm without feeling bulky,” she told us in our guide to the best winter jackets for women . ( Note: Van Delen was a brand ambassador for Oysho at the time of publication .)

“Sometimes it just takes a scarf, a cool brooch or one wild print to make your usual clothes feel new again,” Des Awofeso, a style strategist and content creator, said when asked for wardrobe upgrade tips . She recommended this 100% silk scarf, a sensible gift for the sharpest dresser you know to wear any season.

This bag’s “light weight, flight-friendly size and built-in shoe compartment make it a perfect travel buddy”, wrote Karen Yuan, Filter US commissioning editor, in our guide to the best Nordstrom anniversary sales . We’ve confirmed that a smaller Baboon to the Moon bag could fit three weeks’ worth of things, so imagine the extra days this bag lets you pack for. A great gift for anyone planning holiday travel (including yourself).

We work for our readers, rather than the brands we write about. Every item we recommend is something a Filter editor or contributor has personally tested and can vouch for. On occasion, we may present a close substitute from the same brand and explain any differences from the original.

We scour competing retailers for the lowest prices on any item, and we use price-tracking tools such as Camelcamelcamel to check price history and ensure the discounts are truly good value, rather than a faux markdown. So, while we love sharing good deals, we also aren’t afraid to call out the unimpressive ones, such as this sale on one of our favorite cast-iron skillets , which looks appealing at nearly $30, but actually often sells at that price at various retailers. (For our review of that skillet and other non-toxic pans, read here .)

articles.title: Commonwealth Games 2026: opening ceremony in Glasgow – as it happened
articles.summary: “The friendliest Games in the friendliest city in the friendliest nation” were officially opened, with competition beginning on Friday Admittedly I’ve been fasting since last evening – it’s what Jews call Tisha B’Av – but all this talk of Glasgow has me chaleshing for a deep fried Mars bar. A deep fried anything, really, I’m not fussy, but I’d take a Mars all day long. It’s not just athletics, though. I’m really looking forward to the netball, which should be an Olympic sport but isn’t, and also the swimming. We’re missing Summer McIntosh, but we’ve got Chad le Clos, looking to break the Commonwealth medals record, and Mollie O’Callaghan – among others, Continue reading...
articles.content:
That being the case, this is it from me. Thanks all for your company; we’ll be back tomorrow, and with us, we’re bringing … sport! But until then, love and peace.

Oh, and in a style not inappropriate for Glasgow, things end abruptly; that’s our lot until tomorrow.

Nathan Evans and the St PHNX Band return, noise increasing, and I think this might be the finale; TNT are showing us the schedule for tomorrow, which is extensive. There’s not much I love more than working-day sport, and it’s back with us, from tomorrow.

I dunno, I think they might’ve shown a bit more imagination in getting in party acts, but there are obviously lots of moving parts to negotiate. Some dancers arrive to up the ante, but we need a fuller sound that can be delivered with a single guitar.

Ha, now it’s into No Scotland, No Party, a bit more like it, then her big hut; she’s doing her best to get it going.

Well, sort of – Leave A Light On isn’t exactly a floor-filler but it does get livelier at the end, I’m not sure I’ve seen her since Glastonbury 2005, when every time I switched on, she was playing Suddenly I See.

World In Union strikes up and I’m back at the 1991 rugby World Cup, then Tom Walker emerges. It’s another slow number, but surely we’re getting a drop of some sort at some point?

He speaks about the “unique and extraordinary family of nations” then moves on to plastic pollution and the desire to protect the oceans, before segueing into warm words for Glasgow. He thanks the city, the athletes, the organiser and the volunteers, hoping for a harmonious and durable future, before declaring the Games open … to a rapturous response.

Meantime, the baton moves around the stage, then the King’s speech is removed from inside it, having been placed there on Commonwealth Day, and now it’s time for that.

This is a moody, downbeat version; I wonder if it’ll kick off at some point.

Those were two very nice speeches, and now we’re charting the passage of the batons, before Nina Nesbitt feat. Madrigirls sing the Scottish classic I’m Gonna Be (500 Miles). I wonder what Proclaimers think about their tune becoming a second national anthem; what a feeling that must be.

Next, it’s on to the elements, music on streets, celebrations in parks and celebrations in every neighbourhood, “the energy and spirit that makes this city special … the friendliest Games, in the friendliest city, in the friendliest nation, because that’s who we are.”

The chairman of the Games, George Black. applauds Glasgow for taking them on, praising his city’s confidence for getting together and making things work. “You will see Glasgow deliver, again,” he says.

“Somewhere in this beautiful city, a young person will watch one of you athletes and quietly decide, one day – one day – that will be me.”

Then, to finish, “Compete fiercely, be generous in victory, gracious in defeat, but above all, wear your colours with price and make lifelong friends.” Amen.

He’s glad to be back in Glasgow and gladder to be in Wallace tartan, reimagined in the colours of Uganda, his country. He thanks Glasgow for stepping in as host, celebrating the coming together of nations and stories, sport bringing people together in a world that can seem divided. Every flag is equal, everyone part of something bigger than themselves – “this is what Commonwealth sport is about, not just competition but connection.”

Now it’s speech time, with Dr Donald Rukare, the president of Commonwealth Sport.

The flag is raised, after which comes further marching, then it’s time for the Games oath – all about respect, shared humanity, friendship and diversity.

The Commonwealth Sport flag is carried in by athletes representing the various continents and para-sport, then passed to various armed forces types – not my cup of Irn Bru, if you’re asking – and marching doth ensue.

Yes Sir, I Can Boogie is where we go next, and rightly so; if there’s one thing Glasgow knows how to do, it’s have a party.

The rapper Eve is on next, to a backdrop of those gorgeous old buildings in town, with a message of diversity, “THESE ARE OUR NEIGHBOURS”, written across them as various ethnicities parade. After which it’s time to finish our day at the famous Sub Club – the sound there is meant to be very, very serious

On the screen at the back of the stage, the words “People make Glasgow” – and almost everywhere else, then a singer with whom I’m unfamiliar.

“St Lucia” begins Ger Nugent. “The only country named after a female?”

Well that was nice, and now we’ve three tour guides taking us back in time to the early days of the rag trade, then the famous shipyards – which spawned, among others, Alex Ferguson, followed by wind turbines.

Now we’re people-watching under the clock in Glasgow Central train station, a meeting point since the early part of the 20th century, with Nathan Evans and The Saint PHNX playing their hit Home.

Cheers greet its end, and now I’m steaming we’ve no sport to follow immediately. But instead, we’re getting a day in the life of Glesga and we see a mural of Scott McTominay scoring against Denmark with “Choose Scotland” on it; what a life that boy is living, can there be two better places to be a hero than Scotland and Naples?

And that completes the walk-in; now, we’ve the actor Sam Heughan searching Nessie. Surely, shirley, the orchestra razz out this classic.

Cited for filming it, Hoy explains: “I’m still a fan at heart.” Six Olympic golds and this is where his heart is at, enjoying the joy of others; a mensch.

..and enter Scotland! Alex “Tattie“ Marshall, a bowler at his eighth Games, leads them out, and the noise level increases; for this athletes, it’s getting real.

In come Jersey, then Malta, which leaves us with … Northern Ireland, Scotland and Wales.

Next up it’s Gibraltar, then Guernsey, husband and wife bowlers, Ian and Alison Merrien, with the flag. Then Isle of Man, with Alfie Bezance, a para-powerlifter the youngest flag-bearer at 17.

And finally it’s Europe, starting with Cyprus, then England, the flag carried by bowler Sian Honnor with swimmer Luke Greenbank holding the baton. “I can’t explain the pride to know that you’re leading your team out and you’re ready to go into battle,” bravehearts Chris Hoy.

Tonga arrive, then Tuvalu, whose capital Funafuti is one I’ll never forget as it’s one I brought into school when a kid in my year said he’d learnt them all – he had – then Vanoatu complete the continent.

Now come Samoa, who haka in front of the royal box, and Solomon Islands, whose blend of blue, yellow and green is a delight.

New Zealand are out waving ferns, then Niue, then Norfolk Island – an Australian territory 1600km north-east of Sydney measuring 8km x 5km.

Australia emerge in blazers – it seems impossible they won’t top the medals table – followed by a very chill-looking Cook Islands, their women wearing garlands on their heads, great stuff. Fiji are next, then Kiribati, an archipelago in the central Pacific, and Naoeru, the world’s third-smallest country now known by its indigenous name rather than Nauru.

Trivia time: what is unique about St Lucia? Trinidad & Tobago come next, then Turks & Caicos complete the Caribbean delegation, meaning it’s time for Oceania.

Grenada are followed by Jamaica, then it’s Montserrat, whose black tracky has bright orange and green stripes; I’m interested. Then it’s St Kitts & Nevis – I turned my blood to Ting while working there – then St Lucia, then St Vincent & The Grenadines.

Antigua and Barbuda precede Barbados, mourning the wonderful Garfield Sobers, also in varsity jackets but with lovely blue and yellow. British Virgin Islands are next, then Cayman and Dominca.
paragraphs: That being the case, this is it from me. Thanks all for your company; we’ll be back tomorrow, and with us, we’re bringing … sport! But until then, love and peace.
Oh, and in a style not inappropriate for Glasgow, things end abruptly; that’s our lot until tomorrow.

Nathan Evans and the St PHNX Band return, noise increasing, and I think this might be the finale; TNT are showing us the schedule for tomorrow, which is extensive. There’s not much I love more than working-day sport, and it’s back with us, from tomorrow.

I dunno, I think they might’ve shown a bit more imagination in getting in party acts, but there are obviously lots of moving parts to negotiate. Some dancers arrive to up the ante, but we need a fuller sound that can be delivered with a single guitar.
Ha, now it’s into No Scotland, No Party, a bit more like it, then her big hut; she’s doing her best to get it going.

Well, sort of – Leave A Light On isn’t exactly a floor-filler but it does get livelier at the end, I’m not sure I’ve seen her since Glastonbury 2005, when every time I switched on, she was playing Suddenly I See.
World In Union strikes up and I’m back at the 1991 rugby World Cup, then Tom Walker emerges. It’s another slow number, but surely we’re getting a drop of some sort at some point?

He speaks about the “unique and extraordinary family of nations” then moves on to plastic pollution and the desire to protect the oceans, before segueing into warm words for Glasgow. He thanks the city, the athletes, the organiser and the volunteers, hoping for a harmonious and durable future, before declaring the Games open … to a rapturous response.

articles.title: Seth Meyers on Trump’s approval ratings: ‘Worse than Bush during the Iraq war’
articles.summary: Late-night host discussed Trump’s claims of a rigged 2020 election, push for voting changes and new Canadian tariffs Last night, Seth Meyers focused on Donald Trump ’s claims that China swayed the 2020 election, new tariffs on Canadian imports and his attempt to impose voting restrictions. Continue reading...
articles.content:
Late-night host discussed Trump’s claims of a rigged 2020 election, push for voting changes and new Canadian tariffs

Last night, Seth Meyers focused on Donald Trump ’s claims that China swayed the 2020 election, new tariffs on Canadian imports and his attempt to impose voting restrictions.

On Late Night with Seth Meyers, the host reacted to reports that Trump’s approval ratings in his second term are the worst of any 21st-century president, scoring lower than Barack Obama and George W Bush .

“Trump is doing worse than Bush during the Iraq war – and remember, Bush had a pair of shoes thrown at him,” Meyers said.

“That was the moment you could tell Bush played baseball. He reacted to [dodging] that first shoe like, ‘That’s inside.’

“Trump, on the other hand, has zero agility. If someone threw a shoe at him, he’d still have a mark. And the sole of the shoe would be a mess! Or just perfectly painted.”

Meyers pointed out that Trump is unpopular with people with no party affiliation as well as those on the left. According to a Washington Post poll, he is scoring worse with independents than Richard Nixon during Watergate.

Reacting to a CNN host who called the numbers “quite troubling”, Meyers laughed: “Quite troubling? I would say that’s a real fucking understatement.

“That’s like if your doctor came into the exam room with an X-ray and said, ‘As you can see here, you have 27 Matchbox cars lodged in your rectum. Compounding the matter, you ate lettuce tainted with cyclospora, which means the cars are blocking the explosive diarrhea.’”

In a speech last week, Trump accused China of illegally acquiring 220m US voter files in a pattern dating back to the 2020 election.

“This happened in 2020, you say?” asked Meyers. “Man, whoever was president back then must feel like an idiot right now.

“Also – I’ve never asked this of a president before – are you drunk, bro? You sound like you’re 10 beers deep at a frat party complaining about your girlfriend’s besties.”

While Trump continues to insist that the 2020 election was rigged, Meyers pointed out that much of the voter data that he says China stole is in the public domain or available to be legally purchased from states for a small fee.

“Yeah, of course you can – you can buy anything online,” said Meyers. “And by the way, not only is all that information already public, but Trump’s own team of cranks and nutjobs admitted after the speech there was still no proof of widespread election fraud.”

This week Trump also announced plans for steep tariffs on Canadian imports with up to 50% fees on goods including cement and hockey sticks.

“Hockey sticks?” asked Meyers. “So you’re just a total dick. You might as well slap a tariff on Molson, maple syrup and the concept of apologizing. That is such an insult to Canada .

“It’s like Canada imposing a tariff on something that’s quintessentially American … like gambling on crypto while you sit on the toilet because you made the mistake of thinking it was safe to eat lettuce from Taco Bell .”

Trump is also demanding that Congress pass a law that would require voters to show their passport or birth certificate at polling stations, in a move that would disenfranchise millions of people.

“Who the hell knows where their birth certificate is?” asked Meyers. “I’m guessing mine’s in a manila folder somewhere between takeout menus and photos of my brother”.

articles.title: My first relationship just ended. How can I expect to find someone else when it took 15 years to find her? | Leading questions
articles.summary: To stay open-hearted and optimistic, you need hope, writes advice columnist Eleanor Gordon-Smith . That’s not the same as expectation Read more Leading questions I’m nearly 30 and my first serious relationship just ended a month ago. It lasted eight months, she was my first girlfriend and first kiss, etc. She broke up with me in a very kind and respectful way, and I have been … OK, I guess. The thing is, everyone has been saying to me, “But you must feel so much more confident now.” I don’t. My self-esteem and confidence wasn’t exactly soaring before I met her, but now I feel even less so. It just feels like most people are less tolerant or accepting of my shortcomings. Continue reading...
articles.content:
To stay open-hearted and optimistic, you need hope, writes advice columnist Eleanor Gordon-Smith . That’s not the same as expectation

I’m nearly 30 and my first serious relationship just ended a month ago. It lasted eight months, she was my first girlfriend and first kiss, etc. She broke up with me in a very kind and respectful way, and I have been … OK, I guess.

The thing is, everyone has been saying to me, “But you must feel so much more confident now.” I don’t. My self-esteem and confidence wasn’t exactly soaring before I met her, but now I feel even less so. It just feels like most people are less tolerant or accepting of my shortcomings.

If this wonderful, understanding person couldn’t see me as worth it, or enough or whatever, how can I expect to find someone else when it took 15 years to find her?

Eleanor says: A person declining or ending a relationship with you is not a scorecard on your value. I know it sure feels like it.

But it’s really important to separate the pain of being rejected from the pain of being proven “not worth it”, because although they feel nearly identical, only one has actually happened.

People end relationships because of the relationship itself: they felt you were not a fit with them. The same trait that makes you not fit with one person can make you a perfect fit with someone else. And in the search for love, that’s what we’re really after: not approval, not having lots of people like us, nice though that would be. We want to find a relationship that fits . The fact this lovely person felt you didn’t quite fit together isn’t evidence of your objective value, any more than learning that your neighbour doesn’t like porridge would convince you that porridge is bad.

The bad news is, we can’t predict if or when we’ll find that fit. You asked: “How can I expect to find someone else when it took 15 years to find her?” and I can’t give you that. I can’t give you proof that you can expect to find someone else.

But do you need it? To stay open-hearted and optimistic, you need the hope, not the expectation, you’ll find someone.

It might feel daunting to do the things that would help you find someone new – putting yourself out there; giving new connections the benefit of the doubt – when you’re not even sure they’ll work. But nobody’s sure they’ll work. Hope can be the motivation where expectation can’t be. You don’t need to know that it’ll work out. You can be led by the possibility that it might.

In fact, that’s what everyone has to do in the search for love, no matter how many relationships they’ve had. Don’t think of yourself as unlucky in love, such that you alone have to subsist on hope when everyone else gets to dine on sumptuous certainty. A very long romantic history doesn’t get you across the line to knowing you’ll find a relationship, or even that the one you’re in will last. Everyone, no matter how historically well-liked or beautiful or charismatic, faces ineliminable uncertainty that can only be bridged by choosing to pay attention to the possibility that love might work out instead of the possibility that it won’t.

Remember, too, that self-esteem and esteem from others have to be separable if either is to last. You said you feel like “most people” are less accepting of your “shortcomings”. You might be right, but double-check to make sure it’s not you who’s withholding the acceptance you want. Often we feel things are happening relationally when in fact – or as well – they’re happening within us.

Other people’s love and acceptance is worth wanting – everybody wants to feel cherished. But try hard to separate uncertainty about whether you’ll find that with another person from whether you can feel it for yourself. Relationships are about finding a fit; self-esteem is about recognising worth. Neither one can replace the other.

articles.title: The roaring 20s: how the current decade revolutionised cinema
articles.summary: Pre-Covid, the movie landscape was dramatically different: YouTubers weren’t dominating the box office, video game spin-offs rarely made an impact and Winnie-the-Pooh wasn’t yet the star of a slasher movie. articles.content:
Pre-Covid, the movie landscape was dramatically different: YouTubers weren’t dominating the box office, video game spin-offs rarely made an impact and Winnie-the-Pooh wasn’t yet the star of a slasher movie. Why such a flurry of innovation – and what will be next?

C inema has always been a medium of research and development. At any given point, the industry is searching for yet another breakthrough that can drive it into the future. Synchronised sound in the 1920s. Technicolor in the 1930s. The invention of the modern blockbuster in the 1970s.

Conversely, the consensus is that, if the 2020s has a defining trend, then it is one of decline. The theatrical experience is under attack from all angles: streaming, YouTube , a once-in-a-century health crisis that shut cinemas down entirely. And yet, if you look closer, this has actually been one of the most innovative decades that cinema has ever seen. Whether down to technology or desperation, the film industry has spent these last few years figuring out new ways to make films that simply could not have existed even at the turn of the decade.

Currently, the most notable of these is the way that cinema has become a conversation with YouTube. Back in 2005, when Jawed Karim uploaded the first video ( the 19-second Me at the Zoo ) to the platform, YouTube felt like a novelty. It was cheap and cheerful, a collection of cat videos and people falling over. How things have changed. Not only has it become the largest streaming platform in the world, but the first generation of adults who grew up with YouTube has come of age.

As we speak, the seventh and eighth biggest films of 2026 have been directed by people who learned their trade making content for YouTube. Obsession ( worldwide gross: $442m ) was directed by Curry Barker, a 26-year-old who dropped out of film school to make YouTube videos and TikToks. Meanwhile, Backrooms ( worldwide gross: $384m ) is even more explicitly internet-inspired. Directed by the then 20-year-old Kane Parsons, the film is based on a series of shorts he began uploading to YouTube in 2022, which in turn were inspired by some creepypasta (web-based horror content) that has been floating around online since 2019.

A decade ago, studios would have got this disastrously wrong. Ask them to adapt a YouTube video and they would have hired Michael Bay and Rihanna to make David After Dentist: The Motion Picture ; it would have baffled anyone who didn’t watch YouTube – and appalled anyone who did. This is the first time that the industry has trusted natives to make the films they want to make. Clearly, given the enormous financial returns, it will not be the last. Steven Spielberg is now producing Alex Kister’s The Mandela Catalogue , based on a terrifying analogue horror series that has run on YouTube since 2021.

This would also be a good time to mention the rise of the copyright expiry movie. In 1998, the Copyright Term Extension Act was passed in the US, which extended protection to works to 95 years after they were first published. (In the UK, the rules are different; copyright extends to 70 years after the creator’s death.) This effectively meant that two decades went by without anything going out of copyright. When things finally caught up in 2019, freeing up anything made in 1923, there was a goldrush.

Now, let it be said that it wasn’t a particularly good goldrush. First out of the blocks was 2023’s Winnie-the-Pooh: Blood and Honey , a low-rent slasher movie whose only original thought was to have Winnie-the-Pooh in it. Then came The Mouse Trap and Screamboat (both based on Steamboat Willie, the first element of the Mickey Mouse character to have lost copyright protection ), along with Popeye the Slayer Man and Bambi: The Reckoning . Without exception, these films are cheap, exploitative and burn out their premise long before the end credits – but more are to come.

Next year, if you can wait that long, will see the release of something called Poohniverse: Monsters Assemble , which will star – deep breath – Winnie-the-Pooh, Tigger, Bambi, Peter Pan, Tinker Bell, Pinocchio, Sleeping Beauty and the Cheshire Cat. It is also worth pointing out that King Kong enters the public domain in 2029, which will be brilliant news for anyone who has been longing to see a low-rent horror movie starring an unusually large gorilla.

Now, here is where I may lose you, because I am about to name Sonic the Hedgehog as the most important movie of the decade. Please bear with me. One of the most pervasive trends of this decade has been the way that, for good or ill, audiences have hijacked movies to their own ends. The biggest example is obviously Barbenheimer , in which Barbie and Oppenheimer were released on the same day, kickstarting a phenomenon where people bought tickets to see one straight after the other. Nobody in the film industry expected this, but the publicity from this grassroots celebration drove receipts of both films through the roof.

The flipside to this is Morbius , a terrible film that inspired no end of ironic online mockery – but what was hilarious was that Sony mistook the irony for sincere support, and rereleased the film on 1,000 screens to cash in. It goes without saying that nobody watched it. Also, think of all the extra teenagers who bought tickets for Minions: The Rise of Gru, purely so they could watch dressed in suits and babble nonsense to participate in the Gentleminions trend , or the people who went to see A Minecraft Movie exclusively to scream and chuck stuff about when Jack Black said the words “chicken jockey” .

No film, arguably, has benefited from fan reaction quite like Sonic the Hedgehog. The first we saw of it was a nightmarish trailer featuring an unrecognisable main character. Sonic had bizarrely human proportions, as well as tiny eyes and teeth that looked as if they had been stolen from someone’s mouth while they slept. It was awful, and the internet wasted no time in saying that it was awful. In times gone by, studios would have simply taken the hit and released the film as quietly as possible, writing it off as another godawful video game adaptation destined to die on impact. But this time, producers listened. The trailer was released on Tuesday. By Thursday, the director had tweeted that the character would be completely redesigned. Six months after that, New Sonic was unveiled , and fans were delighted.

With a character that people could actually stand to look at, Sonic the Hedgehog went on to become the sixth biggest film of 2020. Nobody saw this coming. Until Sonic, video game adaptations were universally written off as the lowest form of cinema. Think of the dismal live action Super Mario Bros movie , or Mark Wahlberg’s baffling Max Payne , or Uwe Boll’s irredeemable Postal . About 46 English language video game adaptations were released between 1993 and 2019, and they made a combined total of $5.4bn. But A Minecraft Movie made close to a $1bn on its own. The animated Super Mario Movie released in 2023 ended the year second only to Barbie. At time of writing, The Super Mario Galaxy Movie is the biggest film of 2026 so far worldwide. By demonstrating to other film-makers how to give people what they actually want to see, Sonic has turned the video game adaptation into a licence to print money.

As you will agree, this makes Sonic the Hedgehog by far the most important film of the decade. And the future will only bear this out further, once Mr Beast signs on to direct Sonic the Hedgehog 5 and someone makes a rubbish horror film about a blue hedgehog, as soon as the rights lapse in 2087.

articles.title: Rachel Roddy’s ratatouille and potato gratin recipe
articles.summary: The classic Provencal stew, but with potato for ballast and topped with a cheesy herb crumb We swapped the heatwave and melting pavements in Rome for a similar situation in London, which meant catching a train to Dorset felt something of a relief. Not that it wasn’t hot there too, but it was offset by the sea, a garden and a dark, cool house. The weather also meant that the always good July produce was superb and abundant: all the berries, cherries, peas, runner and french beans; but also potatoes, aubergines, courgettes, tomatoes and herbs – all of which go into this week’s recipe. My template for ratatouille is a mix of Jane Grigson’s recipe in her Vegetable Book and Robert Carrier’s version in Great Dishes of the World, plus the advice from a French friend to cook the aubergine separately, either by deep-frying (by far the tastiest option), baking, or air frying. The benefit of this step is not just flavour; it ensures the aubergine holds its shape better, meaning distinct chunks in the final stew. Continue reading...
articles.content:
The classic Provencal stew, but with potato for ballast and topped with a cheesy herb crumb

W e swapped the heatwave and melting pavements in Rome for a similar situation in London, which meant catching a train to Dorset felt something of a relief. Not that it wasn’t hot there too, but it was offset by the sea, a garden and a dark, cool house. The weather also meant that the always good July produce was superb and abundant: all the berries, cherries, peas, runner and french beans; but also potatoes, aubergines, courgettes, tomatoes and herbs – all of which go into this week’s recipe.

My template for ratatouille is a mix of Jane Grigson’s recipe in her Vegetable Book and Robert Carrier’s version in Great Dishes of the World, plus the advice from a French friend to cook the aubergine separately, either by deep-frying (by far the tastiest option), baking, or air frying. The benefit of this step is not just flavour; it ensures the aubergine holds its shape better, meaning distinct chunks in the final stew.

What I like about Grigson’s recipe is the initial lively simmer (with a lid on), which kickstarts the cooking, followed by an uncovered simmer, then a final livelier simmer so any wateriness disappears. You will have a good idea of how sweet or acidic the tomatoes are from tasting them raw, but taste again towards the end of cooking and, if necessary, add a pinch of sugar or jot of red-wine vinegar.

Ratatouille is a Provençal word, which, according to the digital dictionary Orthodidacte, is linked to two terms: tatouiller, which means “to handle repeatedly” or “grope,” as well as “to stir in water’; and ratouiller , which means ‘to stir in water,” “mix for a long time,” or “stir up the mud at the bottom of the water”. I am at a loss as to how “grope” can be applied here, but the rest of the terms can be applied in some way to the making of ratatouille, especially the stirring of the mud at the bottom of the water, which is a reminder to swirl the spoon deeply so the vegetables don’t clump and stick to the bottom of the pan.

Potatoes are delicious when simmered along with tomatoes until soft, but their addition does turn it into another dish, which is why I have called this ratatouille and potato gratin – the gratin being a topping of soft breadcrumbs, grated cheese, minced herbs and olive oil, which, under the grill, turns into a crusty top.

If you are eating straight away, let the dish sit for about 10 minutes, so the juices that have run free are reabsorbed, before serving with sausages, grilled cheese, slices of roast chicken or simply a green salad. While the top will lose some of its crusty edge, the dish is just as good and even more velvety at room temperature.

1 large aubergine, cut into 2cm chunks Olive oil Salt 1 large onion, peeled and sliced 1 garlic clove, peeled 2 courgettes, cut into chunks 1 red or yellow pepper, cut into chunks 1 all purpose large potato, peeled and cut into chunks 3 big tomatoes, peeled, or 1 x 400g tin tomatoes Sugar and red-wine vinegar (optional)

For the topping 100g of soft bread crumbs 2 tbsp grated parmesan 2 tbsp minced herbs (parsley, thyme, marjoram, say)

Either deep-fry the aubergine chunks until golden, or spread them on a baking tray, rub them with oil and salt and bake for 25 minutes, until soft and golden, or use an air fryer. Set aside.

In a heavy-based pan, warm four to six tablespoons of olive oil and the onion and garlic. After about five minutes, add the courgettes, peppers and a pinch of salt, and stir. Five minutes later, add the potato and tomato, cover and simmer for 20 minutes.

Check the amount of liquid and add a little water so that the vegetables are surrounded, then reduce to a simmer for 40-45 minutes, stirring every now and then.

Ten minutes before the end of cooking, raise the heat a little so wateriness bubbles away for a dense stew. Then, in the last minutes of cooking, stir in the aubergine and taste for salt, adding a jot of sugar or red-wine vinegar, if you wish. Leave to cool a little.

Tip the stew into a roughly 30cm x 20cm rectangular dish. Combine the topping ingredients with some olive oil, then spread the crumbs over the top of the ratatouille and put under the grill for a few minutes, until the top is crusty.

articles.title: Charles Gaines, whose book Pumping Iron helped to popularize bodybuilding, dies at 84
articles.summary: 1974 book led to making of seminal documentary and introduced Arnold Schwarzenegger to the world Charles Gaines, the author whose book and documentary Pumping Iron helped bring professional bodybuilding into the mainstream and introduce Arnold Schwarzenegger to the world, has died. He was 84. Gaines died on Tuesday, according to Greta Gaines, the author’s daughter. She did not reveal a cause. Continue reading...
articles.content:
1974 book led to making of seminal documentary and introduced Arnold Schwarzenegger to the world

Charles Gaines, the author whose book and documentary Pumping Iron helped bring professional bodybuilding into the mainstream and introduce Arnold Schwarzenegger to the world, has died. He was 84.

Gaines died on Tuesday, according to Greta Gaines, the author’s daughter. She did not reveal a cause.

“You always marched to your own drummer and inspired everyone around you to do the same,” she wrote in a tribute to her father. “Follow that bliss, catch that fish and kiss those lips while you can because my father was the live life to the fullest kinda guy.”

Gaines collaborated with George Butler to write Pumping Iron: The Art and Sport of Bodybuilding, which led to the making of the documentary Pumping Iron, for which Butler and Gaines wrote the script.

In the introduction to their 1974 book, Gaines wrote that he and Butler considered it “a sort of respectful report from the interior of an interesting, colorful and heretofore ignored region of American life”.

In a 2022 edition, he admitted that neither author thought “many people outside of the bodybuilding world would be interested in” the book. Gaines wrote that Schwarzenegger almost single-handedly “brought bodybuilding out of the shadows it had languished in since its earliest days in America and into the limelight”.

Schwarzenegger, who won seven Mr Olympia and five Mr Universe titles and who was pictured flexing on the cover of Pumping Iron, harnessed the attention into a career, first in films – including The Terminator, True Lies, Twins and Predator – and then politics, becoming governor of California from 2003 to 2011.

Gaines wrote that he had been involved in bodybuilding in one way or another since he was 16, when he began lifting weights. Pumping Iron began as an article for Sports Illustrated in 1972 on a bodybuilding contest for Mr East Coast.

“If we felt at times a little like 19th century explorers, it was because we found bodybuilding to be as primeval and unmapped as parts of Labrador,” he wrote. “Nobody, we discovered, had been back into it to send out a report on what it was like.”

Gaines and Butler used the book as the basis for their 1977 documentary Pumping Iron, which centers on the rivalry between Schwarzenegger and fellow bodybuilder Lou Ferrigno in the competition for a Mr Olympia title. Ferrigno later starred on TV in The Incredible Hulk.

Gaines was raised in Alabama and earned a bachelor’s degree from Birmingham-Southern College and a master of fine arts in writing from the University of Iowa.

Gaines published numerous books throughout his career, including Waters Far and Near: Tales of Angling Adventure and Misadventure Around the World and the novels Stay Hungry, Dangler and Survival Games. Stay Hungry was adapted into a movie starring Jeff Bridges, giving Schwarzenegger his first movie role.

“He climbed mountains, trekked across ice, fished, and hunted on almost every continent, writing about every adventure in a way that captured people’s hearts and minds. He was interested in everything – and his interest was contagious,” Schwarzenegger wrote on Instagram.

“I’m lucky that he was always looking for new frontiers, because that’s how he found me and our niche sport of bodybuilding.”

In addition to his daughter Greta, Gaines is survived by his wife, the artist Patricia Ellisor Gaines, and two other children, Latham and Shelby Gaines.

“You always marched to your own drummer and inspired everyone around you to do the same,” she wrote in a tribute to her father. “Follow that bliss, catch that fish and kiss those lips while you can because my father was the live life to the fullest kinda guy.”
Gaines collaborated with George Butler to write Pumping Iron: The Art and Sport of Bodybuilding, which led to the making of the documentary Pumping Iron, for which Butler and Gaines wrote the script.

In the introduction to their 1974 book, Gaines wrote that he and Butler considered it “a sort of respectful report from the interior of an interesting, colorful and heretofore ignored region of American life”.

In a 2022 edition, he admitted that neither author thought “many people outside of the bodybuilding world would be interested in” the book. Gaines wrote that Schwarzenegger almost single-handedly “brought bodybuilding out of the shadows it had languished in since its earliest days in America and into the limelight”.

Schwarzenegger, who won seven Mr Olympia and five Mr Universe titles and who was pictured flexing on the cover of Pumping Iron, harnessed the attention into a career, first in films – including The Terminator, True Lies, Twins and Predator – and then politics, becoming governor of California from 2003 to 2011.

Gaines wrote that he had been involved in bodybuilding in one way or another since he was 16, when he began lifting weights. Pumping Iron began as an article for Sports Illustrated in 1972 on a bodybuilding contest for Mr East Coast.

“If we felt at times a little like 19th century explorers, it was because we found bodybuilding to be as primeval and unmapped as parts of Labrador,” he wrote. “Nobody, we discovered, had been back into it to send out a report on what it was like.”

Gaines and Butler used the book as the basis for their 1977 documentary Pumping Iron, which centers on the rivalry between Schwarzenegger and fellow bodybuilder Lou Ferrigno in the competition for a Mr Olympia title. Ferrigno later starred on TV in The Incredible Hulk.
Gaines was raised in Alabama and earned a bachelor’s degree from Birmingham-Southern College and a master of fine arts in writing from the University of Iowa.

Gaines published numerous books throughout his career, including Waters Far and Near: Tales of Angling Adventure and Misadventure Around the World and the novels Stay Hungry, Dangler and Survival Games. Stay Hungry was adapted into a movie starring Jeff Bridges, giving Schwarzenegger his first movie role.

“He climbed mountains, trekked across ice, fished, and hunted on almost every continent, writing about every adventure in a way that captured people’s hearts and minds. He was interested in everything – and his interest was contagious,” Schwarzenegger wrote on Instagram.
“I’m lucky that he was always looking for new frontiers, because that’s how he found me and our niche sport of bodybuilding.”
In addition to his daughter Greta, Gaines is survived by his wife, the artist Patricia Ellisor Gaines, and two other children, Latham and Shelby Gaines.

articles.title: How to turn beetroot leaves into a classic south Indian vegetable dish – recipe | Waste not
articles.summary: Simple spices, fresh coconut and a handful of lentils or nuts is enough to transform leaves normally destined for the compost Poriyal is south India’s version of sauteed spiced vegetables, and it can be made with just about any vegetable, though green beans or a leafy green such as spinach, moringa or amaranth are most commonly used. The vegetables are fried with onions and spices, a spoon of urad dal is added for texture (if you don’t have urad dal, a handful of cashews makes a delicious alternative), and it’s finished with a grating of fresh coconut. This fragrant, spicy and flavoursome side dish also works brilliantly with the earthy flavour of beetroot leaves, and I like to serve it with boiled beetroot, yoghurt, chapatis or/and rice. Continue reading...
articles.content:
Simple spices, fresh coconut and a handful of lentils or nuts is enough to transform leaves normally destined for the compost

Poriyal is south India’s version of sauteed spiced vegetables, and it can be made with just about any vegetable, though green beans or a leafy green such as spinach, moringa or amaranth are most commonly used. The vegetables are fried with onions and spices, a spoon of urad dal is added for texture (if you don’t have urad dal, a handful of cashews makes a delicious alternative), and it’s finished with a grating of fresh coconut. This fragrant, spicy and flavoursome side dish also works brilliantly with the earthy flavour of beetroot leaves, and I like to serve it with boiled beetroot, yoghurt, chapatis or/and rice.

I’ve adapted this recipe from the incredible Maunika Gowardhan , author of Curry , one of the New York Times best cookbooks of 2025. She writes: “The key to cooking spinach poriyal is to let the greens cook without any water. As they wilt and release their moisture, they begin to take on the flavour of the spices.” Beetroot leaves, which feature in a lot of Indian recipes, cook just like spinach leaves, and I like to use the stalks, too, not least because they impart a lovely red colour to the dish. It’s best to add them with the onions to make sure they cook through. I love the addition of freshly grated coconut at the end, and bought one especially; it is optional, however, and can be replaced with rehydrated desiccated coconut.

1 bunch beetroot leaves (roughly 160g), or another leafy green (spinach, kale, cabbage) 1½ tbsp neutral oil , or ghee 1 tsp black (or white/yellow) mustard seeds 1 dried red chilli (optional) 6-10 fresh curry leaves , or to taste 2 tsp ur id dal , or 1 small handful crushed cashews 1 small onion , peeled and roughly chopped ¼-½ tsp chilli powder 35g freshly grated coconut , or 10g desiccated coconut, pre-soaked in a little warm water for 10 minutes 1 handful fresh coriander , chopped from stem to leaf

To serve (all optional) Boiled beetroot , cut into wedges Yoghurt Chapatis or/and rice

Wash the bunch of beetroot leaves and strip the leaves off the stalks. Shred the leaves and cut the stalks into 1cm lengths.

Put a wok or frying pan on a medium heat, add the oil or ghee and, once it’s hot, stir in the mustard seeds and optional dried chilli. When the seeds start to pop, add a few curry leaves and the urad dal or a handful of crushed cashews, then add the chopped onion, beetroot stems and chilli powder to taste. Saute, stirring, for five minutes, or until translucent, then add the leaves and saute until wilted. Season to taste, then stir in the freshly grated coconut with a few more curry leaves and a handful of chopped coriander, and serve.

articles.title: ‘I come from a murderer’: the woman who found out Charles Manson was her grandfather
articles.summary: Difficult new Hulu documentary follows a 26-year-old woman’s startling discovery that she’s related to the notorious, murderous cult leader Sophia Maddox spent her life hearing from her father about how he had never met his father. He would tuck her into bed as a child, she said, and tell her about how much he wanted to know him. Four years ago, following a match on a genealogy website, they finally learned the shocking truth: her father’s father – her grandfather – was Charles Manson , the notorious cult leader and criminal. Continue reading...
articles.content:
Difficult new Hulu documentary follows a 26-year-old woman’s startling discovery that she’s related to the notorious, murderous cult leader

Sophia Maddox spent her life hearing from her father about how he had never met his father. He would tuck her into bed as a child, she said, and tell her about how much he wanted to know him.

Four years ago, following a match on a genealogy website, they finally learned the shocking truth: her father’s father – her grandfather – was Charles Manson , the notorious cult leader and criminal.

“When my dad found out, he couldn’t bring himself to tell me for two months because it was so dark,” Maddox told the Guardian.

This discovery is the subject of Maddox’s documentary , My Grandfather Charles Manson, which premieres on Hulu . Throughout the documentary, Maddox works through complex feelings around being related to one of the most infamous criminals in history. “I come from a murderer,” she says in the first few seconds of the film. “Who the fuck would accept me?”

But what begins as almost a philosophical exercise on the origins of evil, with Maddox seeking to understand her grandfather through interviews with members of the Manson Family cult, evolves quickly into a practice of unpacking generational trauma and making peace with a legacy inherited from flawed figures.

It’s a highly personal process that feels akin to sitting in on a therapy session, which Maddox is filmed doing several times throughout the film. But the decision to document this for an audience came almost instinctually, Maddox said.

“I grew up kind of lonely and when you grow up pretty isolated and lonely, you want to be heard and seen. I think it nurtures you to want to tell people and shout from the rooftops who you are and what your story is,” Maddox said in an interview. “That’s just how I was built and raised, to be a storyteller, and maybe hopefully this allows someone else to feel a little less lonely.”

Maddox, 26, is young enough that she was able to grow up away from the shadow that Manson – and the bizarre trial of the Manson Family members – has cast over the collective psyche of American culture. She only learned of his crimes when she was a teenager and Googled “Helter Skelter” by chance after listening to the Beatles’ White Album, but she didn’t truly comprehend the impact of his crimes until a few years later, when she watched Quentin Tarantino’s bloody blockbuster, Once Upon a Time … in Hollywood .

As the svengali-like leader of the Manson Family cult, in August 1969 Manson had instructed his followers of mostly disaffected young women to enter the home that the actor Sharon Tate, who at the time was eight and a half months pregnant, shared with her husband, Roman Polanski (Polanski had been shooting a movie in Europe). There, they brutally stabbed and killed Tate, alongside the Hollywood hairdresser Jay Sebring, heiress Abigail Folger and family friend Wojciech Frykowski. The followers also shot and killed a fifth person, houseguest Steven Parent. The next night, the Manson Family broke into the home of the supermarket executive Leno LaBianca and his wife, Rosemary, and stabbed them to death as well.

Manson and three female followers, Susan Atkins, Patricia Krenwinkel and Leslie Van Houten, were convicted of murder and conspiracy to murder, with another follower, Charles “Tex” Watson, convicted later. Manson died in prison in 2017 at the age of 83.

Throughout the documentary, Maddox and her co-director, Alexandra Orton, juxtapose happy home videos of her father, Daniel Arguelles, laughing and doting on her as a chubby-cheeked baby, with clips of Manson, raving and wild-eyed, a Nazi swastika carved into his forehead. The physical similarities between Manson and Arguelles are striking – they share the same sharp features and penetrating brown eyes – but throughout the film, Maddox grappled with other traits Manson shared with her father: childhood trauma, mental health issues, a certain unpredictability that could manifest in volatile ways.

Arguelles, who Maddox described as a great dad, had been conceived during a one- or two-night stand and was raised by his mother and stepfather, who sent him to military reform school when he was young. “My grandma, God bless her, rest in peace – I knew her as a kid, and she kept the secret to her grave,” Maddox said. “But when she was a mother to my father, she didn’t treat him the way he deserved to be and I think it was because of who his dad was … the biggest trauma that I could think of with my dad was how no one validated his experiences in childhood, and so that’s why he felt the need to tell me about them when I was a kid.”

The deeply empathetic Maddox also clearly struggled with doing right by her grandfather’s many victims while also humanizing someone who has been depicted for decades as a symbol of pure evil. She had to suss through a lot of sensationalism surrounding her grandfather to really get to the root of what she was searching for. “I’ve made a lot of true-crime documentaries and I think society has this fascination with making monsters that are superhuman that could be in a superhero movie as the villain,” Orton said. “But if you want to understand how to stop bad things from happening, you have to understand that every monstrous thing is done by a human being.

“My therapist has been great for me and in the movie, she says that inheriting sensational wounds is not how you break generational traumas,” Maddox added. “I just had to find out what I had to break.”

The ultimate lesson Maddox learns on screen is that while evil cannot be passed on generation to generation, trauma can, and that was what her father passed on to her. Arguelles also had a very difficult time with the discovery that Manson was his father – as well as facing the similarities that they shared. “I never knew him, but I know him. I am him, but I’m not him,” he said in the documentary. A film-maker and creative himself, he supported his daughter’s quest to explore their background through film, but worried about how healthy it was for her to do so. Tensions came to a head in a hard-to-watch confrontation Maddox has with her father that she captured on film, and she hasn’t spoken to him since.

“You know, for all the vulnerability and openness that I have, I really learned it from my dad because he was quite lonely too … I learned how to be vulnerable and open because my dad was to me growing up,” Maddox said.

Maddox said she hoped to reconcile with her father one day and that he himself will take the time to watch the documentary – to understand that with the generational trauma, he also passed on goodness to her.

“I hope he feels seen,” she said. “I hope he feels seen because he lived a lot of his life with people turning a blind eye. I also hope he’s proud of me, that I’ve dug deep into our generational trauma, our mental health generational trauma as well, and I hope he can see that if I am able to get past it, he can too.”

My Grandfather Charles Manson is available now on Hulu in the US and Disney+ elsewhere

articles.title: How AI may drive union-resistant tech workers to the bargaining table
articles.summary: Tech workers are increasingly unionizing, trading Silicon Valley’s myth of exceptionalism for collective bargaining to contest the corporate deployment of artificial intelligence For decades, the technology industry was a fortress that labor unions couldn’t breach. Tech workers already had cushy compensation packages, dream benefits like unlimited vacation and free lunch, and a flat corporate hierarchy that made engineers feel as powerful as their bosses, all of whom dressed down in sneakers and hoodies. So why unionize? Now, that fortress is cracking from the inside. Unions have become increasingly popular for tech employees. After months of mass layoffs tied to artificial intelligence and mounting anxieties about how it’s being deployed, some tech workers say they’ve been saddled with higher workloads while facing the threat of job loss caused by the very products they’re building. Workers from Google DeepMind and Meta in the UK are also objecting to how their companies’ AI products are being used, such as for military purposes or to monitor employee productivity . Those same workers are now attempting to unionize. Continue reading...
articles.content:
Tech workers are increasingly unionizing, trading Silicon Valley’s myth of exceptionalism for collective bargaining to contest the corporate deployment of artificial intelligence

F or decades, the technology industry was a fortress that labor unions couldn’t breach. Tech workers already had cushy compensation packages, dream benefits like unlimited vacation and free lunch, and a flat corporate hierarchy that made engineers feel as powerful as their bosses, all of whom dressed down in sneakers and hoodies. So why unionize?

Now, that fortress is cracking from the inside. Unions have become increasingly popular for tech employees. After months of mass layoffs tied to artificial intelligence and mounting anxieties about how it’s being deployed, some tech workers say they’ve been saddled with higher workloads while facing the threat of job loss caused by the very products they’re building. Workers from Google DeepMind and Meta in the UK are also objecting to how their companies’ AI products are being used, such as for military purposes or to monitor employee productivity . Those same workers are now attempting to unionize.

On the frontlines of AI’s rollout in the workplace, tech workers have often been the first to feel its effects – including job loss, employee surveillance , increasing workloads, and uses that conflict with employees’ values. These problems aren’t unique to software companies, though, and how Silicon Valley workers fare could serve as a bellwether for what’s coming as AI spreads to other industries.

A Meta employee who was part of a recent union drive in the UK said, “It’s about us having a voice in all of the operations of the company, from products to employee experience.” The employee spoke to the Guardian on the condition of anonymity for fear of reprisal from their employer.

After Meta cut thousands of workers in May and piloted a program tracking their activities, some employees felt power had tipped toward their employer. Forming a union, the Meta employee said, “is the best way to actually show up as owners, and take our seat at the table”.

The push toward tech unionization has been building for years and includes workers at the crowdfunding platform Kickstarter and Microsoft-owned gaming company ZeniMax, as well as the Alphabet Workers Union (AWU), which represents more than 1,400 workers across Google, YouTube and Waymo.

The latest AI-driven developments will drive more organizing efforts, AWU predicts. In the past year, participation in collective actions like AWU’s job security petition , which garnered 4,500 signatures and was delivered to executives last week, increased. Total union actions at Alphabet doubled in 2025 from the year prior.

“In the age of AI, workers need a real say within our companies now more than ever,” said Emma Jackson, senior staff program manager and AWU communications chair. Strong participation “shows workers’ increasing willingness to take a stand and strengthen their agency”.

In a statement, Google DeepMind said it was in the “early stages” of the process to recognize the union and plans to “continue to engage in good faith”. Meta didn’t comment.

As companies continue to cut, tech workers face a stark reality sharply at odds with the job security they once took for granted. “When I first started in tech, you could leave a job and have another one by the week with better pay,” said John Chadfield, a former tech worker who co-founded United Tech and Allied Workers (UTAW), the UK’s only union specifically for tech industry workers. Now, engineers are realizing they don’t have as much power as they once thought, he said. “You might be well paid, but you are still workers.”

“This sense of privilege and immunity for tech workers – that is gone,” said Simone Robutti, a member of Tech Workers Coalition, an international grassroots tech labor network that’s been growing as companies restructure around AI. “It’s clear that with very few exceptions, tech workers are as replaceable as other workers.”

Meanwhile, employees using AI aren’t necessarily experiencing the efficiency boosts the tech promised to deliver. In a recent survey of information technology workers across the University of California campuses, 65% of people reported taking on more work due to a vacant position. Managers expect AI to fill some of those gaps, but people just end up doing more work, said Max Belasco, a business systems analyst at the University of California, Los Angeles chool of Law and union member. And despite the additional responsibility, only 22% of people said they felt secure in their jobs, the survey showed.

“We want to see the UC embody a new way of approaching technology that isn’t just an echo of the consultant-driven layoffs that you see inside of Silicon Valley or the idea that AI can just be this cost-cutting measure,” he said.

University spokesperson Heather Hansen said the school system was focused on using AI “thoughtfully and responsibly to strengthen its operations”. Decisions about how it uses AI are guided by “operational needs” and “privacy, security, and policy considerations”, she said.

The proliferation of AI is spurring workers outside the tech industry to take a similar tack. National Nurses United, which represents nurses in the US, has fought against the use of AI in clinical settings; Sag-Aftra, which represents workers in media and Hollywood, pushed back on the use of AI to replace creative workers in film.

Unions aren’t the only way to set up these kinds of guardrails, but “when employees begin organizing, it’s often a signal that they don’t feel heard through the channels that already exist,” said Katie Hawkes, an organizational psychologist whose doctoral research focused on tech labor union participation.

She witnessed this during her time at X, where employees spoke up about ethical issues like hate speech on the platform but lacked the means to pressure management. “Ultimately, like every company, leadership retained complete decision-making authority,” she said.

Still, for years, tech workers resisted unions. Chadfield said he started trying to organize tech workers a decade ago, but no one seemed interested. Workers felt reasonably happy, and “in the conversations I was having with people then, it was just like, ‘That’s a ridiculous idea. We don’t need one,’” he said.

Then, in 2018, thousands of Google employees organized to protest against the company’s involvement in Project Maven, a Pentagon initiative that used machine learning to analyze drone footage. The action was surprisingly effective, and Google dropped the contract months later, though Palantir picked it up and continues it to this day. A few years later, in 2021, Google employees formed the Alphabet Workers Union. In 2022, Amazon warehouse workers also formed a union, which the company has fought tooth and nail to not formally recognize.

Within the last few years, though, Chadfield said white-collar workers were increasingly interested in union representation – even at companies like Meta, where he said UTAW did not previously have any members. “Until recently, [Meta] was kind of the very last bastion of that Silicon Valley-ness,” he said. “Now, we’re getting a lot of members from Meta, and that’s not something we ever expected.”

Unions can offer workers significant protections. ZeniMax, the Microsoft-owned game company, unionized in 2023 and finalized a contract for its quality assurance testers in 2025 that included protections relating to AI usage, outsourcing and overtime. In summer, when Microsoft cut thousands of jobs, including some at ZeniMax, severance benefits were spelled out in the contract.

“Building even simple protections over issues like job security is an ongoing effort,” said Autumn Mitchell, a quality assurance tester who was laid off by ZeniMax. “Having a union means having negotiating rights over issues like layoffs, which is more than what a lot of people are working with right now in game development.”

Kickstarter, which formed one of the first company-wide tech unions in 2020, negotiated a new contract in 2025 that included a four-day workweek, a minimum salary for all workers, protections against replacing full-time workers with contractors, and an agreement that management cannot replace roles with AI. Though some employees were skeptical of organizing six years ago, its latest win is an example of what can be achieved with a strong union, Robutti of Tech Workers Coalition Global said. And it’s a lesson that applies beyond the tech industry.

“The best time to unionize was always yesterday,” he said. “The second best time is today.”
paragraphs: Tech workers are increasingly unionizing, trading Silicon Valley’s myth of exceptionalism for collective bargaining to contest the corporate deployment of artificial intelligence

F or decades, the technology industry was a fortress that labor unions couldn’t breach. Tech workers already had cushy compensation packages, dream benefits like unlimited vacation and free lunch, and a flat corporate hierarchy that made engineers feel as powerful as their bosses, all of whom dressed down in sneakers and hoodies. So why unionize?

Now, that fortress is cracking from the inside. Unions have become increasingly popular for tech employees. After months of mass layoffs tied to artificial intelligence and mounting anxieties about how it’s being deployed, some tech workers say they’ve been saddled with higher workloads while facing the threat of job loss caused by the very products they’re building. Workers from Google DeepMind and Meta in the UK are also objecting to how their companies’ AI products are being used, such as for military purposes or to monitor employee productivity . Those same workers are now attempting to unionize.

On the frontlines of AI’s rollout in the workplace, tech workers have often been the first to feel its effects – including job loss, employee surveillance , increasing workloads, and uses that conflict with employees’ values. These problems aren’t unique to software companies, though, and how Silicon Valley workers fare could serve as a bellwether for what’s coming as AI spreads to other industries.

A Meta employee who was part of a recent union drive in the UK said, “It’s about us having a voice in all of the operations of the company, from products to employee experience.” The employee spoke to the Guardian on the condition of anonymity for fear of reprisal from their employer.

After Meta cut thousands of workers in May and piloted a program tracking their activities, some employees felt power had tipped toward their employer. Forming a union, the Meta employee said, “is the best way to actually show up as owners, and take our seat at the table”.

The push toward tech unionization has been building for years and includes workers at the crowdfunding platform Kickstarter and Microsoft-owned gaming company ZeniMax, as well as the Alphabet Workers Union (AWU), which represents more than 1,400 workers across Google, YouTube and Waymo.

The latest AI-driven developments will drive more organizing efforts, AWU predicts. In the past year, participation in collective actions like AWU’s job security petition , which garnered 4,500 signatures and was delivered to executives last week, increased. Total union actions at Alphabet doubled in 2025 from the year prior.

“In the age of AI, workers need a real say within our companies now more than ever,” said Emma Jackson, senior staff program manager and AWU communications chair. Strong participation “shows workers’ increasing willingness to take a stand and strengthen their agency”.
In a statement, Google DeepMind said it was in the “early stages” of the process to recognize the union and plans to “continue to engage in good faith”. Meta didn’t comment.

As companies continue to cut, tech workers face a stark reality sharply at odds with the job security they once took for granted. “When I first started in tech, you could leave a job and have another one by the week with better pay,” said John Chadfield, a former tech worker who co-founded United Tech and Allied Workers (UTAW), the UK’s only union specifically for tech industry workers. Now, engineers are realizing they don’t have as much power as they once thought, he said. “You might be well paid, but you are still workers.”

“This sense of privilege and immunity for tech workers – that is gone,” said Simone Robutti, a member of Tech Workers Coalition, an international grassroots tech labor network that’s been growing as companies restructure around AI. “It’s clear that with very few exceptions, tech workers are as replaceable as other workers.”

Meanwhile, employees using AI aren’t necessarily experiencing the efficiency boosts the tech promised to deliver. In a recent survey of information technology workers across the University of California campuses, 65% of people reported taking on more work due to a vacant position. Managers expect AI to fill some of those gaps, but people just end up doing more work, said Max Belasco, a business systems analyst at the University of California, Los Angeles chool of Law and union member. And despite the additional responsibility, only 22% of people said they felt secure in their jobs, the survey showed.

“We want to see the UC embody a new way of approaching technology that isn’t just an echo of the consultant-driven layoffs that you see inside of Silicon Valley or the idea that AI can just be this cost-cutting measure,” he said.

University spokesperson Heather Hansen said the school system was focused on using AI “thoughtfully and responsibly to strengthen its operations”. Decisions about how it uses AI are guided by “operational needs” and “privacy, security, and policy considerations”, she said.

The proliferation of AI is spurring workers outside the tech industry to take a similar tack. National Nurses United, which represents nurses in the US, has fought against the use of AI in clinical settings; Sag-Aftra, which represents workers in media and Hollywood, pushed back on the use of AI to replace creative workers in film.

She witnessed this during her time at X, where employees spoke up about ethical issues like hate speech on the platform but lacked the means to pressure management. “Ultimately, like every company, leadership retained complete decision-making authority,” she said.

Still, for years, tech workers resisted unions. Chadfield said he started trying to organize tech workers a decade ago, but no one seemed interested. Workers felt reasonably happy, and “in the conversations I was having with people then, it was just like, ‘That’s a ridiculous idea. We don’t need one,’” he said.

Then, in 2018, thousands of Google employees organized to protest against the company’s involvement in Project Maven, a Pentagon initiative that used machine learning to analyze drone footage. The action was surprisingly effective, and Google dropped the contract months later, though Palantir picked it up and continues it to this day. A few years later, in 2021, Google employees formed the Alphabet Workers Union. In 2022, Amazon warehouse workers also formed a union, which the company has fought tooth and nail to not formally recognize.

Within the last few years, though, Chadfield said white-collar workers were increasingly interested in union representation – even at companies like Meta, where he said UTAW did not previously have any members. “Until recently, [Meta] was kind of the very last bastion of that Silicon Valley-ness,” he said. “Now, we’re getting a lot of members from Meta, and that’s not something we ever expected.”

And it’s a lesson that applies beyond the tech industry.
“The best time to unionize was always yesterday,” he said. “The second best time is today.”
